vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological Profile for Lifetime, Daily Oral Administration of Paracetamol (Acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

1. Context and Dose Definition  
• In most clinical jurisdictions the highest dose judged not to present an unacceptable risk in adults is 3–4 g day-1 (≈ 45-60 mg kg-1 day-1 for a 70 kg adult). This is therefore taken as the “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD) for chronic use.  
• The evaluation below assumes that this dose is ingested once or in divided doses every day for the subject’s entire adult life (≈ 70 years).

2. Systemic Availability (ADME)  
Absorption: Rapid and near-complete (> 85 %) from the small intestine (tmax ≈ 0.5–1 h).  
Distribution: Vd 0.9–1 L kg-1 (low‐to-moderate), limited plasma-protein binding (≈ 20 %). Crosses placenta and blood–brain barrier.  
Metabolism:  
• 50–60 % hepatic glucuronidation (UGT1A1, 1A6, 2B15).  
• 30–35 % sulfation.  
• ≤ 10 % CYP2E1/CYP1A2/CYP3A4 bioactivation → N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI), a highly reactive electrophile.  
Excretion: 90–95 % eliminated in urine as conjugates in 24 h; t½ 2–3 h (can lengthen in overdose, liver disease or with chronic use).  
Conclusion: Systemic availability is HIGH but limited by efficient phase-II clearance. With lifelong MTD use the metabolic capacity can be saturated during peaks, increasing NAPQI formation.

3. Chronic Toxicodynamic Activity  
Overall classification: HIGH for liver, MEDIUM for kidney, LOW for other organs (weight-of-evidence).

4. Documented or Predicted Chronic Toxicological Effects  

4.1 Liver

4.1.1 Functional / Clinical Pathology  
• Persistent elevation of ALT, AST, γ-GT; occasional hyperbilirubinaemia.  
• Reduced serum albumin and prolonged prothrombin time in advanced cases.  
• Possible episodes of overt acute‐on-chronic hepatic injury.

4.1.2 Histopathology  
• Focal to pan-lobular centrilobular (zone-3) hepatocyte necrosis or apoptosis.  
• Macrovesicular steatosis, regenerative nodules, bridging fibrosis → micronodular cirrhosis in a subset (especially with alcohol, fasting or CYP2E1 inducers).

4.1.3 Mechanism  
• NAPQI formation depletes hepatocellular glutathione (GSH).  
• Covalent binding to cysteine residues on mitochondrial proteins → oxidative stress, JNK activation, MPT pore opening, ATP depletion → necrotic cell death (Jaeschke & Ramachandran 2018, Toxicol Sci 164: 328–338).  
• Repeated sub-necrotic injury promotes inflammatory-fibrogenic response (Kupffer cell activation, stellate-cell-driven collagen deposition).

Adversity: Adverse.  
Reversibility: Partial—mild enzyme elevations resolve after discontinuation; advanced fibrosis/cirrhosis is irreversible.  
Clinical monitoring: Serum transaminases, bilirubin, INR, ultrasound elastography.

4.2 Kidney

4.2.1 Functional / Clinical Pathology  
• Gradual decline in estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR).  
• Mild proteinuria, sterile pyuria; episodic rise in serum creatinine and BUN.

4.2.2 Histopathology  
• Papillary necrosis; cortical–medullary interstitial fibrosis; proximal tubular degeneration.

4.2.3 Mechanism  
• Renal CYP2E1, CYP3A4 generate NAPQI locally; conjugate depletion in the medulla.  
• Ischaemia secondary to prostaglandin (PG) synthesis inhibition decreases medullary blood flow.  
• Oxidative stress and mitochondrial injury in S3 segment (Stachulski et al. 2021, Arch Toxicol 95: 871–892).

Adversity: Adverse.  
Reversibility: Early functional changes reversible; papillary necrosis/fibrosis largely irreversible.  
Monitoring: Serum creatinine, eGFR, urinary N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase, renal ultrasound.

4.3 Gastro-intestinal Tract

• Dyspepsia, asymptomatic gastritis, rare peptic ulcer bleeding.  
Mechanism: High local concentrations inhibit mucosal PG synthesis albeit less than NSAIDs; oxidative stress from reactive metabolites in gastric mucosa.  
Adversity: Generally mild but can become adverse if bleeding occurs.  
Reversibility: Usually reversible.  
Monitoring: Occult blood, endoscopy if symptomatic.

4.4 Haematological / Immune

• Rare agranulocytosis, pancytopenia, immune thrombocytopenia.  
Mechanisms: Idiosyncratic immune-mediated destruction; potential haptenization by oxidised metabolites.  
Adversity: Adverse.  
Reversibility: Usually reversible on withdrawal; can be life-threatening.  
Monitoring: Periodic blood counts.

4.5 Respiratory

• Very rare interstitial pneumonitis / bronchospasm (mostly hypersensitivity).  
Mechanism: Immunological; not dose-dependent.  
Adversity: Adverse but idiosyncratic and not clearly linked to daily MTD.

4.6 Skin

• Chronic urticaria, fixed-drug eruptions; exceedingly rare Stevens–Johnson syndrome (SJS)/TEN. Idiosyncratic, immune-mediated.  
Adversity: Adverse; not predictable from dose.

4.7 Endocrine / Reproductive

• Mild, reversible decrease in serum testosterone reported in some epidemiological studies; mechanism thought to involve COX-derived PG interference in Leydig cells (Kristensen et al. 2018, Hum Reprod 33: 1747-1757).  
Adversity: Currently considered adaptive/uncertain; reversible.  
Monitoring: Sex-hormone panels if clinically indicated.

4.8 Neuro-toxicological / Auditory

• No convincing evidence of structural neurotoxicity at therapeutic doses.  
• Potential analgesic tolerance (pharmacodynamic adaptation).

4.9 Carcinogenicity

• IARC: Group 3 (not classifiable).  
• Cohort data show weak, inconsistent association with renal cell carcinoma only in heavy, long-term users; mechanism plausibly linked to chronic renal papillary necrosis → mutagenic environment but evidence insufficient.  
Adversity: Uncertain; weight-of-evidence does not confirm carcinogenicity.

5. Overall Weight-of-Evidence Classification

Toxicodynamic activity:  
• Liver – HIGH (robust human & animal data).  
• Kidney – MEDIUM (human epidemiology + animal corroboration).  
• Other organs – LOW (only sporadic idiosyncratic or weak signals).

Systemic availability: HIGH, but detoxication capacity is normally adequate; saturation at MTD peaks drives risk.

6. Adaptive vs Adverse Changes

• Adaptive: Mild-to-moderate hepatic CYP/UGT induction, transient increase in glucuronide excretion; reversible & non-injurious.  
• Adverse: Any hepatocellular necrosis, fibrosis, persistent renal papillary necrosis, serious haematological or cutaneous reactions.

7. Clinical Manageability

• Most serious outcomes are monitorable (liver panel, renal function, blood counts) and early injury is clinically reversible if exposure ceases.  
• Advanced fibrosis/cirrhosis or papillary necrosis are largely irreversible.  
• N-acetylcysteine (NAC) remains an effective antidote if hepatotoxicity is detected early.

8. Data Gaps / Uncertainties

• No controlled lifetime studies in humans—assessment relies on epidemiology, case series, and lifetime rodent studies extrapolated to humans.  
• Individual susceptibility varies with alcohol intake, fasting, malnutrition, genetic polymorphisms (e.g., CYP2E1*1A, NAT2).  
• Possible subtle endocrine or neurodevelopmental effects require further study.

9. Conclusion

Lifelong administration of paracetamol at the current adult MTD is predicted to carry a significant probability of cumulative hepatotoxicity and a moderate probability of nephrotoxicity. These effects are mechanistically explained by bioactivation to NAPQI with consequent oxidative injury in tissues that either generate the metabolite (liver, kidney) or are secondarily affected (GI tract). With vigilant clinical monitoring, early liver and kidney changes are usually reversible; however, progression to chronic liver disease or renal papillary necrosis constitutes irreversible, adverse outcomes.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Chronic Toxicological Profile of Metformin (CAS-No. 657-24-9) after Lifetime Oral Administration at (Human) Maximum Tolerated Dose (≈ 2.0–2.5 g day-1)

1. Systemic Availability and General Toxicodynamic Activity  
   • Oral bio-availability: 50–60 % (saturable absorption via intestinal transporters OCT1/PMAT).  
   • Distribution: rapid, Vd ≈ 1–5 L kg-1, no relevant plasma protein binding; accumulates in liver, kidney, intestine via OCTs/MATEs.  
   • Metabolism: negligible (< 5 %).  
   • Elimination: renal, unchanged, t½ ≈ 4–6 h (extended to 20 h in renal failure).  
   • Toxicodynamic driver: reversible inhibition of mitochondrial respiratory chain complex I → ↓ATP/↑AMP → activation of AMPK.  
   Overall systemic availability = Medium; intrinsic toxicodynamic activity = Low-to-Medium (primarily driven by energy-stress signalling rather than covalent reactivity).  

2. Weight-of-Evidence Data Set  
   • > 60 years of clinical use, > 200 million patient-years; therapeutic window wide.  
   • Key sources: FDA label, EMA EPAR (2018), ECHA registration dossier, PubMed chronic rodent studies (Cheng et al., 2017; Bailey & Turner, 1996), human pharmaco-epidemiology (Lalau & Kajbaf, 2019).  
   • No evidence of genotoxicity or carcinogenicity in two-year NTP bioassays (mice/rats up to 2,500 mg kg-1 day-1; NTP TR-580).  

3. Identified Chronic Effects

3.1 Gastro-intestinal (GI) Disturbance  
   Observation: Persisting nausea, abdominal discomfort, soft stools/diarrhoea in ~10–20 % of long-term users; histologically, mild mucosal hypertrophy/hyperplasia of small intestine in rodents at ≥ 600 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   Mechanism:  
     a. High luminal drug concentrations stimulate enterochromaffin-cell 5-HT3 release.  
     b. Altered bile-acid pool and increased GLP-1 secretion → faster transit.  
   Classification: Adverse when persistent (affects quality of life).  
   Reversibility: Yes—symptoms abate within days after discontinuation or dose reduction.  
   Clinical monitoring: Patient-reported outcomes; stool frequency/consistency; endoscopy not routinely needed.  

3.2 Vitamin B12 Malabsorption → Megaloblastic Anaemia / Peripheral Neuropathy  
   Incidence: 5–10 % reduction in serum B12 ≥ 4 years of therapy; overt anaemia/neuropathy in 0.1–0.5 %.  
   Mechanism: Drug interferes with Ca2+-dependent endocytosis of the intrinsic-factor-B12 complex in the terminal ileum (OCT1-mediated competition).  
   Adversity: Adverse (symptomatic anaemia, neuropathy).  
   Reversibility: Generally reversible with oral/IM vitamin B12 or cessation.  
   Monitorability: Yearly serum B12, holotranscobalamin or methyl-malonic acid (ADA/EMA guidelines).  

3.3 Lactic Acidosis (Very Rare but Serious)  
   Incidence: 3–10 cases per 100,000 patient-years; almost always associated with renal, cardiac or hepatic insufficiency (drug accumulation).  
   Mechanism:  
     a. Inhibition of mitochondrial complex I → ↑NADH/NAD+ ratio → ↑lactate production.  
     b. Suppressed hepatic gluconeogenesis → reduced lactate clearance.  
   Adversity: Severe, potentially fatal.  
   Reversibility: Usually reversible with prompt haemodialysis, bicarbonate and drug withdrawal.  
   Monitorability: eGFR, serum lactate (< 2 mmol L-1), pH, anion gap.  

3.4 Hypoglycaemia (Low Frequency in Monotherapy)  
   Mechanism: Not direct—occurs mainly when caloric intake is low or with other anti-diabetics that increase insulin.  
   Adversity: Adverse.  
   Reversibility: Rapid with glucose intake.  
   Monitorability: Self-monitoring of blood glucose.

3.5 Idiosyncratic Hepatocellular Injury (Rare, case reports)  
   Mechanism: Unclear; likely immune-mediated or mitochondrial idiosyncrasy.  
   Histopathology: Mild-to-moderate mixed (hepatocellular±cholestatic) patterns; no fibrosis.  
   Adversity: Adverse; typically reversible within weeks post-withdrawal.  
   Monitorability: ALT/AST every 6–12 months in high-risk patients.

3.6 Testicular Effects / Endocrine Modulation (High-Dose Rodents)  
   Animal data: Tubular atrophy and reduced spermatogenesis in mice at 1,000 mg kg-1 day-1 (NTP TR-580); slight ↓testosterone.  
   Human evidence: Inconclusive; epidemiology shows neutral or slight beneficial effects on fertility in men with metabolic syndrome.  
   Mechanistic hypothesis: AMPK activation suppresses steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) and 17-β-HSD.  
   Adversity: Not demonstrated in humans at MTD; considered of Low concern with adequate margin of exposure (> 15×).  
   Reversibility: Reversible in rodents after 4-week recovery.  
   Monitorability: Serum testosterone, semen analysis (research setting only).

3.7 Weight Loss / Anorectic Effect  
   Average 1–3 kg sustained weight loss over 1–2 years; largely viewed as beneficial in type-2 diabetes.  
   Mechanism: Altered gut–brain GLP-1 and ghrelin signalling; mild mitochondrial uncoupling.  
   Classification: Adaptive/Beneficial, not adverse.  

3.8 Renal Accumulation (No Direct Nephrotoxicity)  
   • Drug concentrates in renal cortex (OCT2); histology in rodents: minimal epithelial vacuolation without degeneration.  
   • Clinical: No increase in CKD progression; nephrotoxicity not observed.  
   • Risk is functional (drug build-up → lactic acidosis) rather than structural damage.  

4. Effects Not Observed (Negative Findings)  
   • Carcinogenicity: Negative in two-year rodent studies; epidemiology suggests reduced colorectal & liver cancer risk.  
   • Genotoxicity: Negative in Ames, mouse lymphoma, micronucleus, and in vivo chromosome aberration assays.  
   • Teratogenicity: Negative in animals; increasing pregnancy use shows no signal.  

5. Summary Matrix of Effects  

Effect | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically Monitorable?  
GI intolerance | Adverse (quality-of-life) | Yes | Symptom reports  
Vitamin B12 depletion | Adverse | Yes | B12, MMA, CBC  
Lactic acidosis | Adverse (serious) | Yes (if treated) | eGFR, lactate, pH  
Hypoglycaemia | Adverse | Yes | Blood glucose  
Idiosyncratic liver injury | Adverse | Yes | ALT/AST  
Testicular changes (rodents) | Potentially adverse | Yes | Testosterone, semen (if needed)  
Weight loss | Adaptive/beneficial | Not needed | Body weight  
Renal accumulation | Functional risk | Yes (dialysis) | eGFR  

6. Remaining Uncertainties / Data Gaps  
   • Long-term (> 40 years) prospective data on cognitive outcomes given B12 deficit.  
   • Mechanistic understanding of rare hepatotoxicity.  
   • Translation of high-dose rodent testicular findings to sensitive human sub-populations.  

7. Conclusion  
Using a weight-of-evidence approach, metformin at the human maximum tolerated dose exhibits:  
   – Low-to-medium toxicodynamic activity with medium systemic availability.  
   – A narrow spectrum of predictable, monitorable chronic toxicities (GI intolerance, B12 depletion, very rare lactic acidosis).  
   – No evidence of cumulative organ toxicity, genotoxicity or carcinogenicity.  
Most adverse effects are reversible upon dose adjustment or cessation and are readily monitored in a routine clinical setting.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Substance  
• Name (INN): Atorvastatin (synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, CI-981)  
• CAS RN: 134523-00-5 (free acid)  
• Structure: Synthetic, lipophilic HMG-CoA-reductase inhibitor (statin class)

Scenario considered  
• Adult human, oral administration once daily at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for a lifetime (≈ 80 mg/day in clinical practice – the highest approved strength and the dose that produced dose-limiting toxicity in phase-I studies).   
• Continuous exposure is assumed from 18 years of age to natural life-span (≈ 60 years).  

Overall toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent inhibitor of the mevalonate pathway).  
Systemic availability after oral dosing: MEDIUM (≈ 14 % absolute bio-availability, but extensive first-pass extraction → HIGH portal/hepatic exposure; volume of distribution ≈ 380 L, high plasma protein binding).

Weight-of-evidence sources  
1. Human clinical trials and post-marketing pharmacovigilance (FDA/EMA product information, 1996-2023).  
2. Repeat-dose studies in rats, mice, dogs and cynomolgus monkeys (up to 2 years; 10–180 mg/kg/day; Pfizer NDA dossier, cited in EMA EPAR).  
3. Epidemiological meta-analyses of high-dose statin therapy (e.g. LaRosa et al. 2005; Preiss et al. 2011; Collins et al. 2016).  
4. Mechanistic literature on statin myotoxicity and hepatotoxicity (e.g. Thompson et al. 2016; Bouitbir et al. 2020).  

1. Functional and clinicopathological effects seen (or predicted) at lifetime-MTD exposure  

A. Hepatobiliary system  
• Observation: Persistent, dose-related elevations of serum ALT/AST > 3×ULN in ≈ 1 % of subjects on 80 mg/day; rare cases of clinically overt drug-induced liver injury (DILI) (≈ 1/100 000 patient-years).  
• Histopathology (animals, high dose): Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, minimal-to-moderate micro-vesicular steatosis; occasional single-cell necrosis.  
• Mechanism:  
  – On-target inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase in hepatocytes decreases cholesterol synthesis → compensatory up-regulation of CYP- and peroxisome-proliferator pathways → oxidative stress.  
  – Depletion of isoprenoid intermediates (farnesyl-/geranylgeranyl-PP) alters small-GTPase (Ras/Rho) signalling, impairing mitochondrial β-oxidation → steatosis and leakage of transaminases.  
• Classification: Adverse at sustained ALT/AST ≥ 3×ULN; usually reversible within weeks of dose reduction/cessation; routinely monitorable (liver panel every 6–12 months).  

B. Skeletal muscle  
• Observation spectrum: Asymptomatic CK elevation (≈ 3–5 % at 80 mg), myalgia (~2–3 %), myopathy (< 0.5 %), rhabdomyolysis (~0.04 % lifetime risk at 80 mg provided no major CYP3A4 inhibitor co-exposure).  
• Histopathology (biopsies & animals): Type-I fibre necrosis, mitochondrial swelling, myophagocytosis.  
• Mechanism:  
  – Depletion of mevalonate pathway intermediates → ↓Coenzyme-Q10 and ↓prenylated proteins → impaired mitochondrial electron transport, ATP deficiency, Ca²⁺ dys-homeostasis → myocyte apoptosis/necrosis.  
  – Lipophilicity of atorvastatin enhances passive diffusion into myofibres compared with hydrophilic statins.  
• Classification: Adverse once symptomatic or CK ≥ 10×ULN; largely reversible upon discontinuation but rhabdomyolysis can cause secondary acute kidney injury (AKI).  
• Clinical monitoring: Patient-reported muscle symptoms, serum CK, renal function (creatinine, eGFR).  

C. Kidney (secondary)  
• Effect: Pigment nephropathy/acute tubular necrosis due to myoglobin in severe rhabdomyolysis.  
• Mechanism: Myoglobin iron-mediated oxidative injury.  
• Adversity: High (can be life-threatening); reversible in most survivors with supportive care.  
• Monitorable: Serum creatinine, urine output, urinalysis for myoglobin.  

D. Endocrine/Metabolic – Glucose homeostasis  
• Observation: Modest increase in new-onset type-2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) incidence (number-needed-to-harm ≈ 1/250 over 4 years with intensive 80 mg therapy).  
• Mechanism:  
  – Impaired prenylation of GLUT4 trafficking proteins in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue → reduced insulin-stimulated glucose uptake.  
  – Possible β-cell dysfunction via altered isoprenoid/cholesterol pools.  
• Adversity: Adverse (chronic disease); effect appears permanent once diagnosed.  
• Monitorable: Fasting glucose/HbA1c annually; lifestyle intervention mitigates risk.  

E. Nervous system  
• Peripheral neuropathy (rare, < 0.1 %); memory disturbance (< 0.1 %).  
• Mechanistic hypotheses: Cholesterol depletion in neuronal membranes, reduced prenylation of small G-proteins critical for axonal transport.  
• Evidence: Weak; largely case-reports.  
• Reversibility: Usually resolves within months of discontinuation; uncertain in long-term cohort data.  
• Classification: Possible but low-frequency adverse effect; monitor based on symptoms.  

F. Ocular (animal specific)  
• Early rat studies showed increased cataracts at supra-therapeutic doses; no signal in > 20 years of human pharmacovigilance. Considered species-specific, adaptive lens vacuolation due to altered cholesterol in lens fiber membranes.  

G. Carcinogenicity  
• Lifetime studies in rodents (up to 2 years) showed increased liver adenomas in mice and rats only at ≥ 2×HED (human-equivalent-dose). Mechanism: rodent-specific CYP-mediated peroxisome proliferation; not considered human-relevant. No increased cancer risk in pooled human meta-analyses (RR≈1.00).  

H. Reproductive hormones  
• Slight ↓testosterone (~4 %) noted in some men on 80 mg; mechanism: cholesterol is steroid precursor. No clinically meaningful effects on fertility or sexual function in large trials. Effect considered adaptive/compensated.  

2. Adaptive (beneficial) pharmacodynamic effects  
• Marked reduction in LDL-C (≈ 55 – 60 %), modest ↓TG, ↑HDL 5–10 %.  
• Regression of atherosclerotic plaque and ↓cardiovascular events; clinically desirable.  

3. Summary table of key chronic toxicological effects at lifetime-MTD

1. Elevated transaminases  
   – Adverse once > 3×ULN  
   – Usually reversible, monitorable (ALT/AST)  

2. Myalgia / Myopathy / Rhabdomyolysis  
   – Adverse; severity graded by CK  
   – Reversible except for occasional renal sequelae  
   – Monitorable (CK, renal function)  

3. New-onset T2DM  
   – Adverse, likely irreversible  
   – Monitorable (HbA1c, fasting glucose)  

4. Peripheral neuropathy (rare)  
   – Possibly adverse; uncertain permanence  
   – Monitorable by clinical exam/EMG as needed  

5. Hepatocellular hypertrophy/steatosis (histopathology)  
   – Mild changes may be adaptive; progression to necrosis is adverse  
   – Reversible if dosing stopped  

6. Renal AKI secondary to rhabdomyolysis  
   – Adverse; usually reversible with treatment  
   – Monitorable (serum creatinine, urine)  

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Very-long-term (> 30 y) exposure data are limited because the drug was introduced in 1996.  
• Genetic polymorphisms (SLCO1B1 c.521T>C) increase systemic exposure and myotoxic risk – variability not fully predictable at population level.  
• Concurrent medications (CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors) and alcohol intake amplify toxicity; these modifiers were not part of the present theoretical assessment.  

Conclusion  
Atorvastatin administered daily at the human MTD (80 mg) for a lifetime is expected to show a high therapeutic index with substantial cardiovascular benefit, but it carries a defined toxicity profile: dose-related hepatic enzyme elevations, skeletal-muscle injury up to rhabdomyolysis, a modest increase in diabetes incidence, and infrequent neuro-toxic manifestations. Most hepatic and muscular changes are detectable early, are reversible on dose adjustment or cessation, and are manageable via routine clinical monitoring. The diabetes risk represents a non-reversible adverse outcome that must be balanced against the drug’s cardioprotective effects. No compelling evidence exists for chronic renal or carcinogenic toxicity in humans except secondary renal failure after severe myolysis.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted chronic toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for a lifetime where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that is observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile – Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) – Daily administration at the (human) Maximum-Tolerated Dose (MTD) for an entire adult lifetime

1. Introduction and weight-of-evidence approach  
• Prednisone is a synthetic 11-β-hydroxysteroid pro-drug that is rapidly reduced in the liver to prednisolone, the pharmacologically active moiety (Raza & Akhter 2022).  
• The therapeutic window is broad, yet chronic use at the upper end of the tolerated range (≥ 1 mg kg-1 day-1 or ≥ 60 mg day-1 for an adult) is associated with well-characterised, multi-system toxicity.  
• The evaluation below integrates:  
  – Human clinical follow-up studies (e.g. Saag 1999; Fardet 2012; Dixon 2018).  
  – Case-control/longitudinal cohorts.  
  – Experimental animal data (chronic dog/rat studies; FDA review, 2010).  
  – Mechanistic literature on glucocorticoid receptors, HPA axis, bone biology, etc.  
  – Regulatory assessments (EMA EPAR, 2011; US FDA label).  

2. ADME and systemic availability  
• Oral bioavailability: 70–90 % (McGowan 2019).  
• Cmax reached in 1–2 h; rapid hepatic conversion (AUCprednisone ≈ 20 %, AUCprednisolone ≈ 80 %).  
• Plasma t½ prednisolone ≈ 2–4 h; pharmacodynamic t½ 12–36 h via gene-transcription effects.  
• Volume of distribution 0.4–1 L kg-1; 70–90 % albumin/CBG bound.  
• Metabolism: hepatic (CYP3A4, 11-β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase-2); excretion: renal (metabolites).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (well absorbed, systemically distributed, active metabolites).  

3. Toxicodynamic activity  
• High-affinity agonist of the cytosolic glucocorticoid receptor (GR, NR3C1).  
• At supratherapeutic concentrations, weak mineralocorticoid receptor (MR) activity is also expressed.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (pleiotropic, receptor-mediated, affecting almost every organ).  

4. Chronic toxicological effects at lifetime daily MTD  

The table lists the principal organ systems, functional effects, supportive pathology, mechanistic basis, and an expert judgement on adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability.

(For brevity, A = Adverse, Ad = Adaptive, R = Reversible, IR = Irreversible, M = Monitorable)

Organ/Effect | Key findings (functional, clinical pathology, histopathology) | Mechanism | A/Ad | R/IR | Clinical monitoring
-------------|----------------------------------------------|-------------|-------|-------|--------------------
Endocrine/HPA axis | • Suppressed basal & ACTH-stimulated cortisol • Adrenal cortical atrophy on histology | Negative feedback on hypothalamic CRH / pituitary ACTH; GR-mediated apoptosis of zona fasciculata cells | A after chronic exposure (initial suppression adaptive) | R (weeks–months) | AM cortisol, ACTH, Synacthen test
Metabolic / Glucose | • Persistent hyperglycaemia, insulin resistance → steroid-induced diabetes • ↑ Gluconeogenic enzymes (PEPCK) | GR-mediated transcription of gluconeogenic genes; antagonism of insulin signalling | A | Partly R; risk of permanent T2DM in predisposed | Fasting glucose, HbA1c
Lipid profile | • ↑ LDL, ↑ TG, ↑ VLDL; fatty liver (macrovesicular steatosis) | Altered VLDL assembly & lipolysis; up-regulation of SREBP-1c | A | Mostly R | Lipid panel, liver ultrasound, ALT
Fluid & electrolytes / CV | • Na⁺/water retention, oedema, hypertension • Hypokalaemia | Weak MR activation; increased ENaC expression | A | R | BP, serum Na⁺/K⁺, weight
Skeletal – osteoporosis & fractures | • ↓ BMD, trabecular thinning, vertebral & hip fractures • Histology: ↓ osteoblast number, ↑ osteoclast activity; avascular necrosis (femoral head) | 1) ↓ osteoprotegerin, ↑ RANKL → bone resorption 2) Suppressed osteoblastogenesis 3) Reduced sex steroids/Ca²⁺ absorption | A | Partly IR (fractures, AVN); BMD may improve after cessation | DEXA, spinal X-ray, pain imaging
Muscle | • Proximal myopathy, muscle wasting • Histology: type II fibre atrophy | Proteasomal up-regulation (atrogin-1, MuRF-1); inhibited protein synthesis | A | R (months) | CK, physical exam
Immune system | • Lymphopenia, eosinopenia, neutrophilia (demargination) • ↑ Serious infections; poor vaccine response • Lymphoid organ atrophy (spleen, thymus) | Trans-repression of NF-κB, AP-1; apoptosis of lymphocytes | A | Quickly R for counts; infection risk declines within weeks | CBC diff., infection surveillance
GI tract | • Peptic/duodenal ulcers, GI bleeding • Histology: mucosal thinning, micro-erosions | Inhibited prostaglandin synthesis, ↓ mucus, ↑ acid secretion | A | R (ulcers heal) | Endoscopy, faecal occult blood
Liver | • Non-alcoholic fatty liver (steatosis) • Rare idiosyncratic hepatitis | Enhanced de-novo lipogenesis; immune-mediated for hepatitis | A | Steatosis R; hepatitis variable | LFTs, ultrasound
Kidney | • Enhanced GFR initially; long-term risk of nephrolithiasis via bone resorption | Haemodynamic effects; hypercalciuria | A (low severity) | R | eGFR, urinalysis
Skin & connective tissue | • Dermal thinning, easy bruising, striae, poor wound healing • Histology: ↓ collagen I/III, epidermal atrophy | Reduced fibroblast proliferation, impaired TGF-β signalling | A (cosmetic/functional) | Partly R (striae IR) | Skin exam
Ocular | • Posterior subcapsular cataracts • Open-angle glaucoma (↑ IOP) | Lens epithelial oxidative damage; ↑ trabecular ECM | A | Cataract IR; glaucoma manageable | Slit-lamp, IOP
Neuro-psych | • Mood swings, euphoria, depression, psychosis, cognitive deficits • Brain volume decrease in hippocampus (MRI) | GR-overactivation alters monoamines; dendritic remodelling | A | Mostly R, but some cognitive effects persist | Neuro-psych assessment
Growth / reproduction | • Hypogonadism, menstrual irregularities • Fetal growth retardation (if exposure in pregnancy) | Suppressed GnRH, LH/FSH; placental 11-β-HSD2 saturation | A | R after taper | Sex steroids, menstrual history
Carcinogenicity | No convincing increase in de novo tumours in epidemiology. Immunosuppression may enhance secondary malignancy risk (esp. skin, lymphomas) (IARC 2015, Group 3 – not classifiable). | Indirect: ↓ immune surveillance | A (indirect) | n/a | Skin checks, haemogram

5. Overall characterisation  
• Systemic availability: HIGH  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
• Principal targets after lifetime high-dose exposure: bone, endocrine/metabolic axis, immune system, skin/eye, muscle.  
• Many effects are clearly adverse, multi-factorial, and only partly reversible. A few responses (initial HPA suppression, transient demargination neutrophilia) are adaptive but become adverse with chronicity.  
• All major effects are monitorable in the clinic using standard laboratory or imaging techniques. Preventive measures (dose taper, calcium/vitamin D, bisphosphonates, PPI, anti-hypertensives, glucose control) can mitigate but not eliminate risk.

6. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• Lifelong epidemiological data at a strict “MTD” are unavailable; estimates are extrapolated from cohorts on long-term high therapeutic doses (5–20 years).  
• Genetic polymorphisms in GR or metabolising enzymes influence susceptibility but remain poorly quantified.  
• Interaction with co-medications (CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers) can intensify or reduce toxicity.

7. Conclusion  
Prednisone, when taken orally at the maximum tolerated dose every day for life, produces high systemic exposure and exerts high toxicodynamic activity through glucocorticoid and, at high levels, mineralocorticoid receptor engagement. The chronic toxicological picture is dominated by endocrine-metabolic derangements (adrenal suppression, diabetes), severe skeletal demineralisation, immunosuppression, dermal and ocular degeneration, and neuro-psychiatric effects. Most effects are adverse, many are partially reversible on cessation, and virtually all can be monitored clinically, allowing risk-management but not full prevention.

8. Key references  
1. Saag KG et al. “Low-dose long-term corticosteroid therapy in rheumatoid arthritis: an analysis of adverse events.” Ann Intern Med 1999.  
2. Fardet L, Petersen I, Nazareth I. “Risk of fractures in patients receiving systemic glucocorticoids.” Rheumatology 2012.  
3. Dixon WG et al. “Serious infection risk after long-term glucocorticoid therapy.” Arthritis Rheumatol 2018.  
4. EMA. Prednisone EPAR – Scientific discussion, 2011.  
5. US FDA Drug Label – Prednisone, 2010 revision.  
6. Raza K, Akhter M. “Pharmacology of glucocorticoids.” StatPearls 2022.  
7. IARC Monograph 110 (2015) – “Some drugs and herbal products.” (Prednisone not classifiable).  
8. McGowan B et al. “Pharmacokinetics of prednisolone in humans.” Br J Clin Pharmacol 2019.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Chemical identity  
• Preferred name: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD)  
• CAS No.: 1746-01-6  
• Synonyms: “Dioxin”, TetraCDD, Agent Orange contaminant  
• Highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 6.8), practically insoluble in water, very low volatility, extremely persistent (human t½ ≈ 7–11 years).

Systemic availability (expert judgement: HIGH)  
• Oral absorption in mammals ≈ 50–90 % (Rose et al., 1976).  
• Almost complete retention in adipose tissue, liver, skin, and serum lipids; negligible first-pass metabolism; enterohepatic recirculation.  
• Slow metabolic clearance by CYP1A2-mediated oxidation and fecal excretion of conjugates.

Toxicodynamic potency (expert judgement: HIGH)  
• High-affinity ligand for the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR; KD ≈ 10-11 M).  
• AhR/TCDD complex translocates to the nucleus, dimerises with ARNT, and binds dioxin-responsive elements (DREs), altering the transcription of >1 000 genes implicated in xenobiotic metabolism, cell-cycle control, immune regulation, endocrine signalling, lipid/energy homeostasis, and apoptosis.  
• Sustained, pleiotropic dysregulation leads to multiple organ toxicity and carcinogenesis.

Because no deliberate lifetime studies have been performed in humans, the following evaluation integrates:

1. Well-conducted chronic animal bioassays (rat, mouse, hamster, non-human primate).  
2. Epidemiological evidence from accidental/intended industrial exposures (e.g., Seveso, Ranch Hand, Yusho/Yucheng).  
3. In vitro mechanistic data and human physiologically-based TK models.  
4. Weight-of-evidence conclusions by WHO (2005), IARC Monograph 69/100F (2012), US-EPA IRIS (2012 draft update), ATSDR (2022).

“Maximum tolerated dose” (MTD) concept  
• In rodents the chronic oral MTD is in the low ng kg-1 day-1 range because body burden, not external dose, drives toxicity.  
• Scaled to humans the daily “MTD” would give and sustain body burdens ≥150 ng kg-1 (≈3 000 pg g-1 lipid) – well above the highest occupational observations.  
• The discussion below therefore represents the qualitative pattern expected if an adult human were dosed at such a level every day for a lifetime.

====================================================================
1. Carcinogenicity
====================================================================
Observed/expected effects  
• Increased incidence of all-site cancers in epidemiological cohorts (all relative risks 1.3–1.7; Ketchum & Michalek 2005; Consonni et al. 2008).  
• Most convincing animal target sites: liver (cholangiocarcinoma, hepatocellular adenoma/ carcinoma), thyroid follicular cell tumours, lung, skin, oral cavity, and hematopoietic malignancies.

Mechanism  
• Non-mutagenic initiator; acts as a tumour promoter via sustained AhR activation, oxidative stress, epigenetic alterations (DNA methylation, histone modifications), CYP-induced reactive oxygen species, chronic inflammation and endocrine disruption.

Adversity: Adverse.  
Reversibility: Not reversible once neoplastic transformation occurs.  
Monitorability: Yes (periodic imaging, tumour markers, clinical chemistry).

====================================================================
2. Hepatic effects
====================================================================
Functional/clinical pathology  
• Early: Increased serum GGT, ALP, ALT; hepatomegaly (centrilobular hypertrophy).  
• Chronic: Periportal fibrosis, bile-duct proliferation, fatty change, focal necrosis, porphyrin accumulation (uroporphyria).

Mechanism  
• AhR-mediated induction of CYP1A1/1A2, CYP2B and Phase II enzymes increases membrane proliferation (SER), perturbing lipid metabolism.  
• Oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction drive steatosis and fibrosis.

Adversity: Hypertrophy alone may be adaptive; fibrosis, necrosis and porphyria are adverse.  
Reversibility: Hypertrophy reversible after cessation; fibrosis partly reversible; porphyria slowly reversible.  
Monitorability: Serum liver enzymes, bile acids, ultrasound elastography, urinary porphyrins.

====================================================================
3. Metabolic and wasting syndrome
====================================================================
Clinical picture  
• Progressive loss of body weight, muscle wasting, hypophagia, hypoglycaemia, altered lipid profile (hypertriglyceridemia, ↓ HDL).  
• In animals, cachexia responsible for most early deaths at high doses.

Mechanism  
• AhR-dependent suppression of PPAR-α/γ signalling, dysregulated leptin/adiponectin, futile hepatic energy cycling, induction of pro-catabolic cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α).

Adversity: Adverse.  
Reversibility: Partially reversible with cessation and nutritional support.  
Monitorability: Body weight/BMI, serum glucose and lipid panel.

====================================================================
4. Immunotoxicity
====================================================================
Observed  
• Thymic atrophy, decreased CD4/CD8 ratio, suppressed cell-mediated and humoral responses (decreased IL-2, reduced antibody titres to vaccination).  
• Epidemiology: Increased risk of respiratory infections in children exposed in utero; altered leukocyte counts in exposed adults.

Mechanism  
• AhR-driven transcription interferes with NF-κB and STAT5, impairs T-cell maturation, promotes Treg differentiation, inhibits B-cell activation.

Adversity: Adverse (increased infection, impaired vaccine efficacy).  
Reversibility: Partially reversible (thymic atrophy may recover; dysregulated immune memory persists).  
Monitorability: CBC with differential, immunoglobulin levels, vaccine response.

====================================================================
5. Dermatological effects – Chloracne & hyperpigmentation
====================================================================
Clinical manifestations  
• Cystic acneiform lesions, comedones on face, neck, trunk; hyper-/hypopigmented macules; skin dryness.

Mechanism  
• AhR activation of keratinocytes/ sebocytes ↑ CYP1A1 → oxidative damage; altered Notch and EGFR signalling → hyper-keratinisation, sebaceous gland metaplasia.

Adversity: Adverse (scarring, cosmetic impact).  
Reversibility: Lesions may persist for years after exposure; eventually improve.  
Monitorability: Dermatological examination, photography.

====================================================================
6. Endocrine and reproductive effects
====================================================================
Male  
• Decreased serum testosterone, reduced sperm count and motility, testicular atrophy (rodents; limited-quality human data showing ↓ sperm quality).  

Female  
• Irregular menstrual cycles, altered estrogen/progesterone ratio, decreased fertility.

Mechanism  
• AhR competes with estrogen receptors for co-activators; induces CYP1A1/1B1 which metabolise estrogens; alters pituitary GnRH axis.

Adversity: Adverse (impaired fertility).  
Reversibility: Partial after exposure ends; long half-life delays recovery.  
Monitorability: Sex hormone panels, semen analysis, menstrual history.

====================================================================
7. Developmental toxicity (relevant if exposed during pregnancy)
====================================================================
Although the scenario concerns adult dosing, if conception occurs during exposure:  
• Decreased birthweight, learning deficits, enamel hypoplasia, immunosuppression in offspring.  
• Mechanism: AhR-mediated disruption of placental vascularisation and fetal thyroid function.  
Adverse, partly irreversible; monitor by prenatal ultrasound and paediatric follow-up.

====================================================================
8. Thyroid and metabolic hormone disruption
====================================================================
• ↓ total and free T4 with compensatory ↑ TSH; thyroid follicular cell hypertrophy and tumours (rats).  
Mechanism: Induction of UDP-GT causing T4 glucuronidation and increased clearance; direct AhR-thyroid cross-talk.  
Adaptive at low effect (enzyme induction); adverse when sustained hormone imbalance and tumour development occur.  
Monitorable via TSH/T4/T3 assays and thyroid ultrasound.

====================================================================
9. Cardiovascular / metabolic disease
====================================================================
• Epidemiology indicates modest increases in hypertension, dyslipidaemia, type-2 diabetes incidence (US-VA Ranch Hand cohort).  
Mechanism: AhR-dependent endothelial dysfunction, oxidative stress, dysregulation of insulin signalling (IRS-1 phosphorylation).  
Adverse, often irreversible; monitorable by blood pressure, HbA1c, fasting lipids.

====================================================================
10. Bone & tooth integrity
====================================================================
• Decreased bone mineral density, delayed eruption, mandibular incisor defects (rodents).  
Mechanism: AhR interacts with Runx2 and osteocalcin gene regulation; interferes with vitamin-D signalling.  
Adverse, partially reversible; monitor with DXA scanning, dental exams.

====================================================================
11. Neurobehavioural effects
====================================================================
• In adult animals: decreased startle response, learning/memory deficits; limited human data suggest increased risk for mild peripheral neuropathy.  
Mechanism: AhR-dependent disturbance of dopaminergic and glutamatergic pathways; oxidative stress in neurons.  
Uncertain adversity; partial reversibility; monitor via neuropsychological tests, EMG.

====================================================================
Assessment of overall chronic risk at lifetime MTD
====================================================================
• Body burden would progressively accumulate to levels associated with severe multi-organ toxicity, cachexia and increased cancer risk.  
• The spectrum of effects is overwhelmingly adverse, poorly reversible, but clinical progression can be monitored using standard biomarkers and imaging.  
• No adaptive, non-adverse outcome is expected at an MTD sustained for life.

Uncertainties/Data gaps  
• True human MTD unknown; interindividual variability in AhR affinity and elimination half-life (CYP1A2 genotype, body-fat).  
• Epidemiological findings confounded by co-exposures (PCBs, lifestyle).  
• Extrapolation from short-lived rodents to decades-long human exposure involves TK modelling assumptions.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (bioaccumulative).  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent, pleiotropic AhR agonist).  
• Chronic daily oral administration at the MTD would almost certainly lead to progressive, clinically significant, and mostly irreversible adverse health outcomes, dominated by carcinogenicity, hepatic injury, metabolic wasting, immunosuppression, endocrine disruption, and characteristic dermatological lesions.

Key references  
• IARC Monograph 100F (2012) – TCDD carcinogenicity.  
• US-EPA. Reanalysis of Key Issues Related to Dioxin Toxicity and Response to NAS Comments (2012).  
• WHO/IPCS. Evaluation of Certain Food Additives and Contaminants – Dioxins (2005).  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Chlorinated Dibenzo-p-dioxins (2022).  
• Consonni D. et al. (2008) Lancet Oncol. 9: 645-652.  
• Ketchum NS & Michalek JE (2005) J Occup Environ Med. 47: 375-383.  
• Rose JQ et al. (1976) Drug Metab Dispos. 4: 425-428.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Substance evaluated  
• Name: Benzene (synonyms: benzol, cyclohexatriene)  
• CAS No.: 71-43-2  
• Molecular formula: C₆H₆ (aromatic hydrocarbon)  

Summary conclusion  
At a life-time, maximum-tolerated, daily oral dose, benzene shows HIGH systemic availability and HIGH toxicodynamic activity.  The weight-of-evidence from human epidemiology (mainly inhalation), limited oral human data, extensive oral rodent studies, mechanistic experiments and in-silico predictions indicates that chronic oral exposure produces:

1. Persistent, adverse, dose-related depression of the haematopoietic system that can progress to aplastic anaemia, myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) and several types of leukaemia.  
2. Genotoxicity in haematopoietic target cells (chromosomal aberrations, micronuclei, aneuploidy, DNA strand breaks).  
3. Secondary immunosuppression due to lymphoid cell depletion.  
4. Hepatic enzyme induction and limited hepatocellular injury (mainly centrilobular), usually adaptive/reversible at non-leukaemogenic doses.  
5. Rodent-specific forestomach hyperplasia/ulceration (local irritation) with subsequent tumours – considered of low human relevance.  
6. Mild nephropathy and splenic changes secondary to haemolysis/anaemia.  
7. Early-onset CNS depression (narcosis) can occur acutely but usually subsides (tolerance) and is not the primary chronic concern.

Each effect, its histopathology/clinical pathology, mechanistic basis, adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability are detailed below.

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
1. Bone-marrow toxicity → cytopenias → aplastic anaemia  

Evidence  
• Repeated oral gavage in rats/mice (NTP 1986, 1993; concentration-related decreases in WBC, RBC, platelets; hypocellular marrow).  
• Human case reports of accidental ingestion and epidemiological inhalation studies show identical haematological picture (Rinsky 1987; Lan et al. 2004).

Histopathology/clinical pathology  
• Hypocellular marrow with depletion of erythroid, myeloid and megakaryocytic lineages.  
• Peripheral blood: leucopenia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, non-regenerative anaemia, reticulocytopenia.

Mechanism (“why and how”)  
a) ADME: Benzene is readily absorbed (>90 %) from the GI tract, extensively distributed and reaches bone marrow.  
b) Metabolism: Hepatic CYP2E1 (and to lesser extent CYP1A2/2B) forms benzene-oxide → phenol → hydroquinone (HQ), catechol, 1,4-benzoquinone, muconaldehyde. Reactive metabolites are further activated in marrow by peroxidases (myeloperoxidase).  
c) Toxicodynamics:   
   • Reactive quinones generate ROS and covalently bind DNA/proteins in haematopoietic stem and progenitor cells, impairing self-renewal.  
   • Topoisomerase-II inhibition and microtubule disruption cause mitotic arrest and apoptosis (King et al. 1999).  
   • Disruption of stromal cytokine signalling (e.g., reduced CXCL12).  

Adverse/adaptive? → Adverse.  
Reversible? → Partial. Early cytopenias may reverse within weeks–months after cessation; severe aplastic anaemia can be permanent or fatal.  
Monitorable in clinic → Full blood count (CBC) with differential, reticulocytes, bone-marrow aspirate/biopsy; serum erythropoietin; flow cytometry of CD34⁺ cells.

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
2. Leukaemogenicity (AML, ALL, CLL, MDS, NHL)  

Evidence  
• Robust human data (IARC Group 1; NIOSH, OSHA).  
• Oral mouse studies (NTP 1993) show dose-dependent increase in malignant lymphomas and myeloid leukaemias.  

Mechanism  
• Same metabolites as above induce chromosomal translocations (t(8;21), t(15;17), AML1/ETO, etc.).  
• Oxidative DNA damage, epigenetic alterations (hypomethylation of p15INK4B).  
• Clonal expansion of damaged haematopoietic stem cells.  

Adverse/adaptive? → Adverse (severe).  
Reversible? → No once malignant clone is established.  
Monitorable → Periodic CBC, peripheral smear, flow cytometry, cytogenetics; symptoms (fatigue, infections, bruising).

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
3. Genotoxicity (chromosomal and DNA damage)  

Evidence  
• Consistent positive micronucleus, sister chromatid exchange, comet assays in benzene-exposed workers and rodents (Eastmond 2001).  
• OECD TG 474 & 475 in vivo positive after oral dosing.  

Mechanism  
• Covalent binding of benzoquinone/HQ to DNA; formation of depurinating adducts; inhibition of topoisomerase II.  

Adverse/adaptive? → Adverse precursor to cancer.  
Reversible? → DNA damage can be repaired but mis-repair yields permanent mutations.  
Monitorable → Micronucleus assay in peripheral blood reticulocytes; γ-H2AX; urinary 8-OH-dG (research/biomarkers).

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
4. Immunotoxicity  

Evidence  
• Decreased B- and T-lymphocyte counts and serum Ig levels in chronically exposed workers (Lan 2005).  
• Oral mouse studies: lower splenic T-cell proliferative response, thymic atrophy.  

Mechanism  
• Same bone-marrow suppression; plus redox-mediated apoptosis of lymphocytes.  

Adverse/adaptive? → Adverse (predisposes to infection).  
Reversible? → Usually improves after exposure stops if progenitor pool recovers.  
Monitorable → Lymphocyte subset profiling, serum immunoglobulins, vaccine response tests.

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
5. Hepatotoxicity  

Evidence  
• Rats/mice: increased liver weight, centrilobular hepatocyte hypertrophy, minimal necrosis at ≥200 mg/kg-day (NTP).  
• Human occupational data show mild ALT/AST elevation but no overt chronic liver disease.  

Mechanism  
• Adaptive CYP2E1 induction; oxidative stress from reactive metabolites.  

Adverse/adaptive?  
   • Enzyme induction alone → Adaptive, reversible.  
   • Necrosis/fibrosis (rare at tolerated doses) → Adverse.  

Reversibility → High once exposure ceases.  
Monitorable → Serum ALT, AST, ALP, γ-GT; ultrasound if warranted.

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
6. Forestomach irritation/tumours (rodents)  

Evidence  
• Chronic gavage produced squamous hyperplasia, papillomas/carcinomas in mouse forestomach.  

Mechanism  
• Local contact irritation by unmetabolised benzene. Rodent forestomach has no human anatomical counterpart.  

Human relevance → Low (qualitative species difference).  
Adverse in rodents; considered non-relevant to human risk assessment.  
Monitorable in humans → Not applicable.

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
7. Kidney and spleen changes  

Evidence  
• Mild renal cortical tubular degeneration and splenic haemosiderosis secondary to haemolysis in high-dose rats.  

Mechanism  
• Oxidative damage to erythrocytes → haemolysis → iron deposition in spleen; metabolite-induced oxidative stress in kidneys.  

Adverse/adaptive? → Usually mild/adaptive; becomes adverse only at high doses.  
Reversibility → Yes, contingent on haemolysis resolution.  
Monitorable → Serum creatinine, BUN, urinalysis; reticulocyte and haptoglobin levels.

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
8. Neurobehavioural/CNS depression  

Evidence  
• Acute oral doses ≥200-400 mg/kg cause narcosis in rodents; chronic tolerance observed. Occupational inhalation data show headaches, dizziness.  

Mechanism  
• Reversible membrane-fluidising effect of parent benzene on neuronal lipid bilayers and GABAergic modulation.  

Adverse/adaptive? → Generally adaptive/reversible once plasma levels drop; not a chronic limiting toxicity at MTD.  
Monitorable → Clinical neurologic exam, psychomotor tests.

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
Systemic availability (ADME) rating  
• Absorption: High (lipophilic, log Kow ≈ 2.1; complete GI absorption in animals).  
• Distribution: Widely distributes to fatty tissue, bone marrow, liver.  
• Metabolism: Extensive phase-I oxidation (CYP2E1) followed by conjugation; formation of toxic metabolites.  
• Excretion: Metabolites in urine (phenol, catechol, HQ, S-phenylmercapturic acid).  
Overall systemic availability: HIGH.

Toxicodynamic activity rating  
• Multiple mechanism-based toxicities at bone marrow and genome level.  
Overall toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Very limited controlled human oral exposure data; risk extrapolated from inhalation and animal studies.  
• Inter-individual metabolic variability (CYP2E1 polymorphisms, GSTT1 null genotype) not fully accounted for.  
• Long latency between exposure and leukaemia makes dose–response at low exposures uncertain.  

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
Clinical surveillance recommendations for chronically exposed individuals  
1. Baseline and 6-monthly CBC with differential and platelet count.  
2. Annual bone-marrow biopsy or aspirate if cytopenias appear.  
3. Periodic liver function tests (ALT, AST).  
4. Urinary benzene metabolites (S-phenylmercapturic acid, trans,trans-muconic acid) to confirm exposure.  
5. Immunoglobulin quantification and lymphocyte phenotyping if infections increase.  
6. Education regarding early symptoms of anaemia, bleeding, infection.

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
Overall toxicological assessment  
Oral lifetime exposure to a maximum-tolerated dose of benzene leads predominantly to irreversible, adverse haematological and genotoxic outcomes culminating in leukaemia. Other systemic effects (hepatic enzyme induction, mild renal changes, neurobehavioural effects) are largely adaptive or reversible. Continuous clinical monitoring centred on haematology provides the most sensitive early detection of toxicity.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological profile: Arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6; most commonly supplied as sodium arsenite, NaAsO₂)    

Executive conclusion  
• Toxicodynamic activity – HIGH (trivalent inorganic arsenic reacts with critical –SH groups and produces multi-site genotoxicity).  
• Systemic availability after oral dosing – HIGH (≈ 80–95 % absorbed, widely distributed, slow urinary clearance).  
• Chronic daily administration at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) would therefore be expected to produce a wide spectrum of ADVERSE, largely irreversible effects, several of which are clinically monitorable.  

1. Absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion (ADME) – weight-of-evidence  
• Oral absorption: 80–95 % in both humans and experimental animals (ATSDR, 2022; WHO, 2011).  
• Distribution: rapid entry into blood, binding to hemoglobin and intracellular proteins; high levels in liver, kidney, skin, hair, nail keratin. Crosses placenta and blood–brain barrier.  
• Biotransformation: sequential oxidation/reduction and biomethylation (As³⁺ → MMA⁵⁺/MMA³⁺ → DMA⁵⁺). Methylation is not a true detoxification step—MMA³⁺ is often more cytotoxic/genotoxic than parent.  
• Elimination: mainly urine (t½ blood ≈ 10 h for As³⁺, but biologically effective t½ in keratinised tissues > weeks).  
→ Systemic exposure will therefore remain essentially life-long under daily dosing.

2. Principal chronic toxicological effects expected at life-time MTD oral exposure  

2.1 Dermatological  
• Hyperpigmentation (“raindrop” melanosis) and palmar/plantar hyperkeratosis (histology: epidermal thickening, dyskeratosis).  
Mechanism: oxidative stress and altered keratinocyte signal-transduction (EGFR, MAPK); arsenite inhibits DNA repair → clonal expansion of altered keratinocytes.  
Adversity: adverse.  
Reversibility: hyperpigmentation partly reversible on cessation; hyperkeratosis slowly reversible, often persistent.  
Monitorability: visual skin inspection, dermatoscopy, biopsy.

• Non-melanoma skin cancers (basal- and squamous-cell carcinoma; Bowen’s disease).  
Mechanism: genotoxicity (chromosomal aberrations, DNA hypomethylation), promotion by chronic inflammation.  
Adversity: adverse, irreversible.  
Monitorability: dermatological examination, histopathology.

2.2 Carcinogenicity at internal sites  
IARC (2012) – Group 1 carcinogen (sufficient evidence in humans) for lung, bladder, and skin; positive associations for liver, kidney and prostate.  
Mechanisms:  
– ROS-mediated DNA damage/strand breaks;  
– inhibition of DNA repair enzymes (PARP, NER proteins);  
– epigenetic dysregulation (global DNA hypomethylation, promoter hypermethylation of tumour suppressors);  
– altered signal transduction (PI3K/AKT, NRF2).  
Adversity: adverse, irreversible.  
Monitorability: imaging, cytology (urinary), tumour markers (limited), regular clinical screening.

2.3 Peripheral and central neurotoxicity  
• Clinical picture: symmetrical sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy, “stocking-glove” paraesthesia, motor weakness; decreased conduction velocity (EMG). Occasional cognitive effects.  
Histopathology: axonal degeneration, demyelination in distal peripheral nerves.  
Mechanism: thiol interaction with mitochondrial enzymes (pyruvate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase) → ATP depletion; oxidative stress in Schwann cells.  
Adversity: adverse.  
Reversibility: partially reversible when exposure ceases early; long-standing axonal loss largely irreversible.  
Monitorability: neurological exam, nerve-conduction studies.

2.4 Haematological  
• Anemia (normocytic or microcytic), leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia.  
Clinical pathology: ↓Hb, ↓Hct, ↓WBC/PLT; bone-marrow biopsy shows hypocellularity or dyserythropoiesis.  
Mechanism: inhibition of haem synthesis enzymes and bone-marrow mitochondrial function; direct marrow cytotoxicity.  
Adversity: adverse.  
Reversibility: usually reversible (weeks–months) after exposure stops.  
Monitorability: complete blood count.

2.5 Hepatotoxicity  
• Elevated ALT, AST, ALP; histology: fatty change, focal necrosis progressing to periportal fibrosis and, rarely, angiosarcoma/cholangiocarcinoma.  
Mechanism: mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress; altered methyl-transfer reactions affecting hepatocyte gene expression.  
Adversity: adverse.  
Reversibility: early steatosis reversible; fibrosis/cancer irreversible.  
Monitorability: liver enzymes, ultrasound/ elastography.

2.6 Nephrotoxicity  
• Clinical: ↓GFR, tubular dysfunction (proteinuria, glycosuria, aminoaciduria), hypertension; late interstitial fibrosis.  
Histopathology: proximal tubular necrosis, interstitial inflammation/fibrosis.  
Mechanism: uptake via phosphate transporters → mitochondrial inhibition, ROS; MMA³⁺ contributes.  
Adversity: adverse.  
Reversibility: functional changes partially reversible; fibrosis irreversible.  
Monitorability: serum creatinine/eGFR, urinary N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase, protein.

2.7 Cardiovascular and metabolic  
• Peripheral vascular disease (“Black-foot disease”), ischemic heart disease, hypertension.  
• Diabetes mellitus type 2.  
Mechanisms: endothelial dysfunction (ROS, NO depletion), epigenetic changes in insulin-signaling genes, β-cell toxicity.  
Adversity: adverse, partially irreversible.  
Monitorability: blood pressure, ABI, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipid panel.

2.8 Respiratory  
• Chronic bronchitis-like symptoms, decreased lung function; increased lung cancer.  
Mechanism: airway epithelial oxidative damage, chronic inflammation.  
Adversity: adverse; cancer irreversible.  
Monitorability: spirometry, imaging.

2.9 Reproductive & developmental (in adults)  
• Decreased sperm count, testicular degeneration; menstrual disturbances.  
Mechanism: endocrine disruption (interference with steroid receptors) and gonadal mitochondrial toxicity.  
Adversity: adverse, partly reversible.  
Monitorability: hormone panels, semen analysis.

2.10 Immunological  
• Suppressed cell-mediated and humoral immunity; increased susceptibility to infection.  
Mechanism: apoptosis of T- and B-lymphocytes, inhibition of NF-κB signaling.  
Adversity: adverse, reversible.  
Monitorability: differential blood counts, immunoglobulin levels.

2.11 Adaptive / homeostatic responses  
• Induction of metallothionein, glutathione synthase, NRF2-regulated antioxidant genes.  
These are ADAPTIVE, beneficial, and rapidly reversible; monitorable indirectly by gene-expression profiling but not required clinically.

3. Weight-of-evidence statement  
Multiple concordant human epidemiological datasets (Taiwan, Bangladesh, Chile), supported by long-term rodent studies (e.g., Waalkes et al., 2003; Tokar et al., 2010) and mechanistic in-vitro data, demonstrate that trivalent inorganic arsenic produces multi-system toxicity and carcinogenicity at doses only modestly above normal environmental exposure. Given high oral bioavailability and slow elimination, a life-time daily MTD would ensure body burdens well within the human database that shows clear adversity.

4. Overall hazard characterisation  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Critical target organs/tissues: skin, peripheral nerves, bone marrow, liver, kidney, vascular endothelium, respiratory tract, pancreas.  
Key endpoints to monitor clinically during chronic exposure:  
• Urinary/blood arsenic speciation (exposure biomarker)  
• Dermatological exam/biopsy  
• CBC, liver/kidney panels  
• Nerve-conduction studies  
• Fasting glucose/HbA1c  
• Blood pressure, ECG  

Any continuation of exposure at or near the MTD cannot be justified ethically because the majority of effects are adverse, many are irreversible or culminate in malignancy, and safer alternatives exist.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Substance evaluated  
• Preferred name: Methylmercury (CH3-Hg+)  
• Synonyms: monomethylmercury cation, methylmercuric ion, methylmercuric chloride (for salt), etc.  
• CAS No.: 16056-34-1 (chloride salt); risk-relevant moiety is the methylmercury cation.  

Summary conclusion (weight-of-evidence)  
Systemic availability = HIGH (≥90 % oral absorption, long half-life ~45–70 d, extensive distribution, active transport into brain).  
Toxicodynamic potency = HIGH.  Principal chronic target is the central and peripheral nervous system; secondary targets are kidney, cardiovascular and immune systems.  At a life-time Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) given daily by mouth, almost all functional changes are adverse, largely irreversible in the CNS, but clinically monitorable.  

1. Kinetics relevant to chronic exposure  
• Absorption: >90 % from gastrointestinal tract in adults (Nierenberg 1973; WHO 1990).  
• Distribution: Forms cysteinyl conjugate that mimics methionine and is carried across membranes by LAT1 and LAT2 amino-acid transporters.  Crosses blood–brain barrier, placenta and accumulates in erythrocytes and brain (brain:blood ≈ 3–6:1).  
• Metabolism: Slow, microbial demethylation in gut and intracellular demethylation generate inorganic Hg2+, which also accumulates.  
• Elimination: Biliary >> urinary; whole-body biological half-life 45–70 days; effective half-life in brain ≈ years.  

2. Observed or predicted chronic effects at an MTD level  

2.1 Central & peripheral nervous system  
Functional findings (human data: Minamata, Niigata, Iraq bread poisoning; animal corroboration):  
• Early: Paraesthesia of extremities and perioral region, dysaesthesia, headache, fatigue.  
• Progressive: Constriction of visual field (“tunnel vision”), impaired colour vision, night blindness, hearing loss (high frequencies), ataxia, tremor, dysarthria, intention tremor, nystagmus, cognitive slowing, mood lability, memory deficits.  
• Late/severe: Spasticity, muscle wasting, rigidity, epileptic seizures, coma.  

Toxicological histopathology  
• Focal neuronal degeneration and loss in granular layer of cerebral and cerebellar cortices (especially calcarine area, visual cortex) (Takeuchi 1977).  
• Loss of large-diameter myelinated axons in dorsal columns and peripheral nerves; Wallerian degeneration.  
• Astrocytosis, microgliosis; decreased synaptophysin; Purkinje cell loss; cerebellar vacuolation.  

Mechanism(s)  
a) Hg–SH binding → inhibition of key seleno- and thiol enzymes (e.g. thioredoxin reductase, glutathione peroxidase) → oxidative stress.  
b) Formation of MeHg-cysteinate mimics methionine → carrier-mediated uptake into neurons.  
c) Microtubule depolymerisation & disruption of axonal transport.  
d) Mitochondrial dysfunction → ATP depletion, Ca2+ dys-homeostasis; facilitation of glutamate excitotoxicity (NMDAR over-activation).  
e) Inhibition of neurogenesis and synaptogenesis (largely demonstrated in animals).  

Adversity: CLEARLY ADVERSE.  
Reversibility: Peripheral sensory symptoms may partially regress after exposure cessation; central neuronal loss is largely IRREVERSIBLE.  
Monitorability: Neurological examination, quantitative sensory testing, nerve-conduction studies, visual‐evoked potentials, audiometry, neuro-psychological testing; brain MRI or advanced imaging may show cortical atrophy.  Biomarkers: total Hg in whole blood, hair or toenails; plasma selenium status (modifying factor).  

2.2 Kidney  
Functional & clinical pathology  
• Low-grade proteinuria, tubular enzymuria (N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase), microalbuminuria; mild rise in serum creatinine at high body burdens.  
Histopathology (animal & limited human autopsy):  
• Proximal tubular cell swelling, granular degeneration, basophilic inclusion bodies; chronic interstitial nephritis in severe cases.  
Mechanism: Intracellular demethylation to Hg2+ accumulates in proximal tubule, binding to metallothionein and other sulfhydryl proteins → oxidative stress and cell death.  
Adversity: ADVERSE.  
Reversibility: Partially reversible if body burden lowered early; fibrotic changes irreversible.  
Monitorability: Urinalysis (β2-microglobulin, protein), serum creatinine, eGFR, imaging by ultrasound.  

2.3 Cardiovascular system  
Observed associations (long-term cohorts of fish-eating populations; Faroes, Kuopio):  
• Elevated blood pressure, attenuated heart-rate variability, increased risk of myocardial infarction (Rissanen 2000; Guallar 2002).  
Mechanistic considerations: MeHg-induced oxidative stress and depletion of NO bioavailability in endothelium; interaction with selenium-dependent antioxidant systems; promotion of lipid peroxidation.  
Histopathology (animal, limited human): endothelial swelling, focal necrosis in myocardium at very high doses.  
Adversity: POTENTIALLY ADVERSE.  
Reversibility: Functional dysregulation may improve if exposure ceases; atherosclerotic lesions permanent.  
Monitorability: Blood pressure, ECG (HR-variability), echocardiography, serum lipids, high-sensitivity CRP.  

2.4 Immune system  
• Mixed immunostimulation and immunosuppression: Increased auto-antibodies (antinuclear, antinucleolar), suppression of T-cell proliferation in vitro; increased susceptibility to infections seen in experimental animals.  
Mechanism: Hg modification of self proteins → neo-antigens triggering autoimmunity; depletion of thiol redox status in immune cells.  
Adversity: ADVERSE (autoimmunity); immunosuppression increases infection risk.  
Reversibility: Auto-antibody titres may fall over months after exposure reduction, but established auto-immune disease can persist.  
Monitorability: ANA panel, immunoglobulin levels, cytokine profiling, lymphocyte subsets.  

2.5 Endocrine / reproductive (adult)  
• Mild, inconsistent changes in thyroid hormones (↓-T3/T4 or ↑-TSH) attributable to binding of Hg to deiodinases; generally subclinical.  
• Possible reduction in male fertility parameters at high exposure (motility, morphology).  
Mechanism: Hg interference with seleno- enzymes (type I & II deiodinase, glutathione peroxidase) and steroidogenic enzymes.  
Adversity: BORDERLINE; generally minimal and adaptive until overt dysfunction appears.  
Reversibility: Likely reversible after body burden reduction.  
Monitorability: Serum TSH/T4/T3, semen analysis.  

2.6 Carcinogenicity / genotoxicity  
Human data inadequate; IARC 2B for methylmercury compounds (possible carcinogen).  Animal chronic studies (mice) show renal tumours associated with sustained cytotoxicity and regenerative proliferation.  Mechanism believed secondary to cytotoxicity and oxidative DNA damage.  
Adversity: ADVERSE, irreversible if tumour develops; currently low weight compared to neurotoxicity.  
Monitorability: No validated early biomarkers; routine clinical follow-up.  

3. Overall classification of systemic availability and toxicodynamic activity  
Systemic availability: HIGH — rapid, efficient oral uptake and persistence (biological half-life >1.5 months).  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH — potent electrophile targeting SH/SeH centres with poor detoxification capacity in CNS.  

4. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No true life-time controlled studies in adult humans; rely on poisoning episodes and high-fish consumers.  
• Variability in individual susceptibility (SNPs in LAT1, glutathione genes, selenium status).  
• Interaction with co-exposures (nutritional selenium, PUFA) not fully quantified.  

5. Practical clinical monitoring during life-time oral MTD exposure  
• Quarterly blood total Hg and hair Hg as exposure metrics.  
• Annual neurological exam, neuro-psychological battery, visual and auditory tests.  
• Semi-annual renal panel (urine proteins, serum creatinine, cystatin-C).  
• Cardiovascular risk assessment every 1–2 y.  
• Auto-antibody screening if symptoms suggest auto-immunity.  

Ethical note  
Intentional administration of a life-time MTD of methylmercury to humans would be unethical.  The evaluation above is a theoretical extrapolation from human poisoning data, epidemiology of high fish consumers, and corroborative animal studies.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Vinyl chloride (VC), chloroethylene, chloroethene  
CAS No.: 75-01-4

Route & scenario: Daily oral administration of the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) for the whole adult lifetime.

Weight-of-evidence sources  
• Key animal feeding/gavage studies: Feron et al. 1979; Til et al. 1991; McConnell et al. 2013.  
• Extensive chronic inhalation data: Maltoni et al. 1981; Drew et al. 1983; IARC 2012 Monograph 100F; EPA IRIS 2000; ECHA 2023.  
• Human epidemiology (occupational inhalation).  
• Mechanistic papers on metabolism, DNA adducts, mutagenicity and angiosarcoma: Guengerich et al. 2010; Swenberg et al. 2011; Klaunig et al. 2021.  
• TK models for oral vs. inhalation: Reitz et al. 1996; Clewell et al. 2001.

Because human oral data are absent, the assessment necessarily extrapolates from (1) oral animal studies and (2) the very large human inhalation database, supported by PBPK modelling. Uncertainties are highlighted in each section.

1. Systemic availability (TK)

Absorption  
• Volatile gas, Henry’s constant ≈ 0.8 atm·m³ mol⁻¹.  
• When swallowed in water or gelatin capsule, ~70–90 % is absorbed across the upper GI mucosa before it volatilises (Feron 1979; PBPK fits).  
• High first-pass extraction in liver (extraction ratio ≈ 0.8 at low doses; saturable > MTD).

Distribution  
• Once in blood, partitions rapidly to perfused tissues; steady-state blood:air ~1.4.  
• No significant bioaccumulation; fat:blood ≈ 2–3; equilibrium within hours.

Metabolism & Reactive Intermediates  
• CYP2E1 (± CYP3A4) converts VC to chloroethylene-oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA).  
• CEO is a short-lived, highly electrophilic epoxide; rearranges to CAA or conjugates with GSH.  
• Detoxication: epoxide hydrolase (to glycol), aldehyde dehydrogenase, glutathione S-transferases.  
• At the MTD, metabolic pathways are partly saturated → disproportionate rise of “free” CEO/CAA.

Excretion  
• CO₂ in expired air (major). Minor urinary metabolites: 2-chloroethanol, thiodiglycolic acid (TDGA).

Overall systemic availability rating: HIGH (oral MTD overcomes first-pass metabolism; reactive metabolites formed in situ in liver and subsequently systemically).

2. Principal chronic toxicodynamic effects at the oral MTD

For clarity the main findings are grouped by target system. Each entry states: evidence, mechanistic rationale, adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability.

A. Hepatic effects (dominant target)

A1. Sinusoidal endothelial damage ➔ hepatic angiosarcoma (HAS)  
• Evidence: Feron 1979 (gavage rats, 1 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ for life, ↑ HAS); Til 1991 (dietary, ↑ HAS & hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)); strong concordance with human inhalation epidemiology (HAS SIR > 1000).  
• Mechanism: CEO/CAA form etheno-DNA adducts (ε-dA, ε-dC) in endothelial cells → Kras mutations → clonal expansion; also chronic oxidative stress and local hypoxia.  
• Adverse? Yes – frank malignancy.  
• Reversible? No.  
• Monitorable? Partly; early endothelial injury/fibrosis detectable by liver ultrasound or MRI elastography; serum VEGF-A, vWF may rise, but no reliable pre-clinical marker for HAS.

A2. Hepatocellular carcinoma & cholangiocarcinoma  
• Same dataset as above. Genotoxic MOA identical; additional role for chronic regenerative proliferation after cell death.  
• Adverse, irreversible, monitorable by liver imaging & α-fetoprotein.

A3. Liver fibrosis / portal hypertension (“toxic cirrhosis”)  
• Rats ≥ 0.25 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ lifelong developed periportal fibrosis, oval-cell hyperplasia (Til 1991). Occupationally exposed humans show focal nodular hyperplasia & splenomegaly.  
• Mechanism: sustained Kupffer-cell activation, ROS, TGF-β1 signalling; secondary to endothelial injury.  
• Adverse; partially reversible if exposure stops early; clinically monitorable (elastography, platelet count, ALT/AST, hyaluronic acid).

A4. Steatosis & mild cholestasis  
• Seen in sub-chronic oral rat studies at ≥ 10 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.  
• Adaptive at first (lipid accumulation), but progresses with fibrosis → adverse.  
• Reversible at early stage; AST, ALT, ALP, γ-GT, ultrasound monitoring.

B. Haematological / vascular system

B1. Thrombocytopenia & impaired coagulation  
• Rats and mice: reduced platelet counts, prolonged PT (Til 1991).  
• Mechanism: marrow suppression by CEO, plus consumption in sub-clinical endothelial lesions.  
• Adverse; reversible within weeks post-exposure; monitorable (CBC, PT/INR).

B2. Non-cirrhotic portal hypertension, splenomegaly, oesophageal varices (humans)  
• Secondary to hepatic vascular lesions; same MOA.  
• Adverse; partly reversible; monitorable clinically.

C. Nervous system

C1. Peripheral neuropathy (sensory; “Vinyl-chloride disease”)  
• Inhalation workers; oral data lacking but PBPK indicates similar internal doses.  
• Mechanism: Axonal degeneration due to CAA interference with axonal transport and mitochondrial function.  
• Adverse; partially reversible; monitorable (nerve conduction velocity, vibration threshold).

C2. CNS depression (functional, no histopathology)  
• High oral bolus doses give transient narcosis; at MTD adaptive and reversible; monitorable clinically.

D. Immune & connective tissue

D1. Scleroderma-like skin changes / acro-osteolysis  
• Only reported after decades of high inhalation exposure; no animal oral data. Inclusion uncertain but plausible at oral MTD if systemic CEO levels match inhalation.  
• Mechanism: microvascular endothelial injury → fibrosis; immune dysregulation (↑ auto-antibodies).  
• Adverse; irreversible in bone; monitorable (X-ray, capillaroscopy).

E. Reproductive & developmental

• Oral MTD studies in rats/mice did not show clear fertility impairment, but increased fetal resorptions at maternal‐toxic doses (Hardin & Manson 1980).  
• Mechanistic link: paternal germ-cell DNA adducts and maternal liver-mediated stress.  
• Adverse, potentially reversible (gonadal turnover); monitorable via reproductive indices.

F. Renal effects

• Mild tubular degeneration and ↑ urinary β₂-microglobulin in rats ≥ 10 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.  
• Mechanism: GSH-dependent delivery of CAA conjugates to kidney; oxidative stress.  
• Generally low severity, reversible; monitorable (serum creatinine, microalbumin, β₂-MG).

3. Summary classification of chronic key effects at oral MTD

High systemic availability → high toxicodynamic activity.  
Most critical adverse outcomes:  
• Hepatic angiosarcoma (high, irreversible)  
• Hepatocellular carcinoma (high, irreversible)  
• Progressive liver fibrosis/portal hypertension (high, partly reversible)  
Moderate-to-high: peripheral neuropathy, immuno-connective tissue effects, haematological changes.  
Low-to-moderate: renal tubular effects, transient CNS depression, early steatosis (adaptive when mild).

4. Data gaps & uncertainties

• Human oral exposure data absent; reliance on route-to-route extrapolation via PBPK (uncertainty ± 2–3-fold).  
• Dietary MTD in animals is lower than theoretical human MTD because of palatability/volatility.  
• Species differences in CYP2E1 activity: mouse > human > rat; scaling introduces additional uncertainty.  
• Early biomarkers for endothelial injury are not fully validated.

5. Overall conclusion

If an adult human were dosed orally at the maximum tolerated level every day for a lifetime, vinyl chloride would be systemically absorbed and metabolised to highly genotoxic intermediates. The weight of evidence predicts:

• High likelihood of fatal liver vascular and parenchymal cancers.  
• Progressive liver fibrosis with secondary haematological and vascular sequelae.  
• Additional, albeit less certain, peripheral neuropathy, connective-tissue changes and mild renal injury.

The majority of these effects are clearly adverse, largely irreversible once established, but many can be tracked in the clinic through standard liver function tests, imaging, nerve-conduction studies and blood counts.
