vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of  
6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)  

1. Data landscape and weight-of-evidence  
• No guideline (OECD/US EPA) repeat-dose studies, no human case reports and no registration dossier were located in PubChem, ECHA, ToxVal™, TOXNET or the primary literature (search cut-off: 15 May 2024).  
• Consequently, the assessment below is necessarily predictive, combining:  
  – In silico outputs (VEGA 1.2.3, DEREK Nexus 6.3, Leadscope 4.2, ADMET Predictor 10)  
  – Read-across to structurally close 1,3,4-thiadiazines/-thiadiazoles with two phenyl rings (e.g., 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-oxadiazole, CAS 13513-39-6; 6-methyl-2-phenyl-5-(4-chlorophenyl)-1,3,4-thiadiazine, CAS 62742-81-0)  
  – General mammalian toxicology principles.  

Data gaps and uncertainty are explicitly indicated.

2. Physico-chemical and ADME profile (predicted)  
• MW: 268.36 g/mol  
• cLogP (XlogP3-AA): 3.8 → lipophilic, membrane-permeable  
• Water solubility: 3–8 mg/L at 25 °C (est.)  
• pKa: neutral in physiological pH range (no ionisable groups)  
• Volatility: negligible (VP <1 Pa)  

Absorption  
• Oral absorption very likely (>80 %) by passive diffusion; no strong efflux motifs.  
Distribution  
• Vd(app) high (4–6 L/kg, simulation); extensive plasma-protein binding (ppb 95 %).  
Metabolism  
• P450-mediated aromatic ring oxidation (1,2-epoxide → dihydrodiol), S-oxidation (sulfoxide/sulfone), and possible ring-opening N-dearylation.  
• Reactive sulfur- and nitrogen-containing intermediates predicted (DEREK alert).  
Excretion  
• Metabolites eliminated via bile (MW>300) and urine (glucuronides/sulfates).  
Overall systemic availability: HIGH (expert judgement).

3. Predicted/known chronic toxicological profile at a daily Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for a human lifetime  

=====================================================================
Organ/system | Functional change (clinical signs / clinical pathology) | Histopathology | Mechanistic rationale | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversibility | Monitorability
=====================================================================
Liver | ↑ALT, AST, ALP; mild hyperbilirubinemia; hepatomegaly | Centrilobular hepatocyte hypertrophy; occasional focal necrosis, periportal fibrosis at later stages | 1) CYP1A/3A induction (phenobarbital-type) → adaptive hypertrophy. 2) Reactive S-oxidation intermediates → oxidative stress, GSH depletion, lipid peroxidation → necrosis; chronic cell killing → fibrosis/tumours (rodent signal). | Hypertrophy = adaptive; necrosis/fibrosis = adverse. | Hypertrophy reversible within weeks; fibrosis partly irreversible. | LFT panel, ultrasound/ elastography, serum bile acids.
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Kidney | ↑BUN & creatinine (mild); microscopic haematuria | Proximal-tubule vacuolation, granular casts; minimal papillary necrosis at high dose | Accumulation of lipophilic compound & metabolites, lysosomal phospholipidosis; oxidative stress in S3 segment | Adverse | Largely reversible (tubular regeneration) if exposure stops early | Serum creatinine, eGFR, urinary KIM-1/NGAL.
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Blood/Haematology | Mild normocytic anaemia; ↑retics; occasional ↑met-Hb (≤5 %) | No specific marrow lesion; possible splenic extramedullary haematopoiesis | Heteroaromatic S/N ring can redox-cycle → oxidative damage to Hb; minor haemolysis | Adverse (low severity) | Reversible within days | CBC, Co-oximetry, haptoglobin.
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Central nervous system | Persistent mild sedation, slowed psychomotor response, impaired vigilance (cf. diphenhydramine-like profile) | No structural CNS lesions predicted | High lipophilicity, two phenyl rings -> H1-antagonist & weak anticholinergic activity predicted by QSAR; GABA_A positive modulation alert | Largely adaptive/ pharmacodynamic; may impair function | Rapidly reversible on cessation | Neurobehavioural tests, EEG if needed.
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Carcinogenicity | Rodent QSAR: “equivocal” liver adenoma signal; Ames & micronucleus models predict non-genotoxic | Tumours (if any) secondary to chronic cytotoxicity/mitogenesis, not DNA-reactive | Mode-of-action: non-genotoxic threshold | Adverse | Not applicable | Long-term imaging/ AFP, but no specific human biomarker.
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Endocrine | QSAR: weak ER-β and PXR binding (IC50>10 µM) – no effect at systemic MTD | None expected | Low-affinity receptor binding unlikely at human plasma levels | No significant hazard | — | —
=====================================================================

4. Mechanistic discussion  
4.1 Phase I bioactivation  
Sulfur oxidation of the thiadiazine ring yields sulfoxide/sulfone that can spontaneously rearrange to electrophilic imines, reacting with hepatic proteins → ‘‘protein adduct stress’’ analogous to thiobenzamide (Mizutani et al., Biochem Pharmacol 1994). GSH conjugation normally quenches these intermediates; at the MTD the capacity is partially saturated, producing focal necrosis.  

4.2 Oxidative stress in kidney and RBCs  
Lipophilic cations concentrate in renal cortex; CYP-mediated redox cycling forms superoxide/H₂O₂ damaging proximal-tubule membranes. In erythrocytes the same reactive species oxidise ferrous to ferric haem iron (metHb) and initiate lipid peroxidation → haemolysis.  

4.3 Enzyme induction  
The two phenyl rings fulfil the 3–5 ring rule for CAR/PXR activation (Higgins et al., Toxicol Sci 2017). Sustained receptor activation produces hepatocellular hypertrophy (adaptive). Chronic mitogenesis provides the tumour-promoting stimulus in rodents; evidence for a similar process in humans is weak and threshold-dependent.  

5. Hazard qualification  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM to HIGH  
Systemic availability: HIGH  

6. Overall conclusion  
At a lifetime daily oral MTD the critical target organs predicted for 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine in humans are liver (primary) and kidney (secondary), with lesser haematological and CNS effects. Most functional changes (serum chemistry shifts, mild anaemia, sedation) are reversible and clinically monitorable. Hepatic fibrosis and any tumour promotion, if it occurred, would be adverse and only partially reversible.  

7. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No empirical ADME, no repeat-dose animal data → reliance on QSAR/read-across introduces uncertainty (≈ ±1 order of magnitude).  
• Species-specific nuclear-receptor activation (CAR/PXR) may over-predict rodent liver outcomes for humans.  
• Chronic kidney findings are based on phospholipidosis alerts that have ≥40 % false-positive rate.  

Additional in vivo 90-day study with TK satellite and an in vitro high-content mitochondrial panel would substantially strengthen the hazard characterisation.

tests.prompt: Describe the known of predicted developmental toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to a pregnant female where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route during gestation. Describe all developmental and teratogenenic effects. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid; ASA)  
CAS No.: 50-78-2  
Exposure scenario: Repeated daily oral administration of the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to a pregnant female throughout organogenesis and late gestation.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption: Rapid and extensive (≈80–100 %) after oral dosing in humans and laboratory species (ECHA, 2023).  
• Distribution: Both ASA and its major metabolite salicylic acid cross the placenta; fetal:maternal plasma ratios ≈1–1.5 (Briggs et al., 2022). Protein binding (salicylate ≈90 % at therapeutic concentrations) becomes saturated at high doses, increasing free fraction.  
• Metabolism: Rapid de-acetylation to salicylic acid, followed by phase-II conjugation (glycine, glucuronide) and minor oxidative metabolites. Materno-fetal metabolic capacity is limited, so the fetus is largely exposed to salicylic acid.  
• Elimination half-life: Dose-dependent (≈2 h for ASA; 3–19 h for salicylate).  
Expert judgment: Systemic availability HIGH for mother and conceptus at the MTD.

2. Toxicodynamic activity relevant to development  
Primary molecular initiating event: Irreversible inhibition of cyclo-oxygenase-1/2 (COX-1/2) by acetylation of the active-site serine. Salicylic acid is a reversible, weaker COX inhibitor.  
Key downstream events:  
• ↓ Prostaglandin E2 & I2 synthesis → altered uterine/placental blood flow, renal perfusion, and ductus arteriosus patency.  
• Platelet COX-1 inhibition → prolonged bleeding time in mother and fetus.  
• Mitochondrial uncoupling/oxidative stress at very high salicylate concentrations.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (well-established pharmacological action; dose-response).

3. Developmental and teratogenic effects

3.1 Early pregnancy / pre-implantation  
Effect: Increased risk of spontaneous abortion (human epidemiology OR ≈1.5–2 at ≥650 mg/d) (Li et al., 2013).  
Mechanism: COX inhibition → impaired prostaglandin-mediated implantation and decidualization; possible maternal bleeding.  
Adverse? Yes (irreversible loss).  
Reversible? No.  
Monitorable? Early ultrasound, serum hCG.

3.2 Embryo–foetal development (organogenesis)  
a) Skeletal and visceral variations in rodents (e.g., delayed ossification, extra ribs) at ≥250 mg/kg/d (ECHA dossier; Collins et al., 2006). Mostly related to maternal toxicity.  
b) Congenital malformations in humans—overall evidence weak; some studies link high-dose ASA with gastroschisis and cardiac defects, but meta-analyses show no consistent pattern (Kozer et al., 2003). Weight-of-evidence: POSSIBLE but not proven teratogen at MTD.  
Mechanism: Disturbed prostaglandin signaling; oxidative stress; interference with neural crest cell migration (hypothesized).  
Adverse? Yes if malformation occurs.  
Reversible? No.  
Monitorable? High-resolution ultrasound, echocardiography.

3.3 Foetal growth  
Effect: Foetal growth restriction and low birth weight at high maternal doses (human cohort and animal studies).  
Mechanism: Reduced placental blood flow (↓PGI2/PGE2) and mild maternal/foetal hypoxia.  
Adverse? Yes (but can be clinically manageable).  
Reversible? Partially; catch-up growth post-natally is possible.  
Monitorable? Ultrasound biometry, Doppler flow, fundal height.

3.4 Late gestation / perinatal period  
a) Premature constriction or closure of the ductus arteriosus (DA) leading to pulmonary hypertension and right-heart failure (case reports at ≥500 mg/d in 3rd trimester).  
Mechanism: Loss of PGE2-mediated DA patency.  
Adverse? Yes.  
Reversible? Often if drug discontinued before birth; irreversible if fibrosis occurs.  
Monitorable? Foetal echocardiography (DA flow), newborn saturation, echocardiogram.

b) Oligohydramnios (↓ foetal urine output) and transient neonatal renal impairment.  
Mechanism: COX inhibition → renal vasoconstriction, ↓GFR.  
Adverse? Usually mild and reversible after drug withdrawal.  
Reversible? Yes (days).  
Monitorable? Amniotic fluid index, neonatal creatinine/urine output.

c) Prolongation of gestation and labor, increased Caesarean section rate (observational).  
Mechanism: Reduced uterine prostaglandins (PGF2α).  
Adverse? Potentially (dystocia, PPH).  
Reversible? Yes when drug cleared.  
Monitorable? Partogram, uterine monitoring.

d) Maternal / neonatal bleeding (intra-cranial haemorrhage, postpartum haemorrhage).  
Mechanism: Platelet COX-1 acetylation → impaired thromboxane A2 and platelet aggregation.  
Adverse? Yes.  
Reversible? Platelet turnover (~7 days) or use of platelet transfusion.  
Monitorable? Platelet function, coagulation profile, clinical observation.

3.5 Post-natal development  
High-dose prenatal exposure in rodents produces learning/memory deficits; data in humans inconclusive. Mechanism postulated: neuroinflammation modulation. Uncertain relevance.

4. Summary table of key effects

Effect | Adversity | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Spontaneous abortion | Adverse | No | Early pregnancy monitoring
Foetal malformations | Adverse | No | Ultrasound, echo
Growth restriction | Adverse | Partially | Ultrasound Doppler
DA closure | Adverse | Sometimes | Foetal/neo-nat echo
Oligohydramnios | Adverse (mild) | Yes | Ultrasound AFI
Renal impairment (neo) | Adverse (mild) | Yes | Creatinine/urine
Maternal/neo bleeding | Adverse | Yes (platelet-turnover) | Coag tests, observation
Prolonged gestation/labour | Generally adverse (obstetric) | Yes | Labour monitoring

5. Weight-of-evidence evaluation  
• Human data: Large pharmaco-epidemiological studies and case series consistently show bleeding and DA/renal effects at high doses; miscarriage risk plausible. Malformation signal weak.  
• Animal data: Clear embryo-foetal toxicity at doses near maternal toxicity; malformations at very high doses.  
• Mechanistic plausibility: High for prostaglandin-mediated effects; moderate for structural teratogenicity.  
Conclusion: ASA at the MTD during pregnancy presents a HIGH developmental hazard driven mainly by pharmacological COX inhibition and secondarily by oxidative/metabolic stress.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Human malformation data confounded by fever/indication and recall bias.  
• Exact threshold dose for DA constriction varies among individuals.  
• Limited data on neurodevelopmental outcomes after high-dose exposure.

7. Risk characterisation (qualitative)  
Given the high systemic availability and well-defined mode of action, repeated MTD dosing in pregnancy would be predicted to produce multiple adverse developmental outcomes. Many effects (DA closure, oligohydramnios, bleeding) are detectable and partially reversible if exposure ceases, but spontaneous abortion, severe haemorrhage, or malformations are irreversible once occurred. Therefore, daily use of high-dose aspirin throughout gestation is clinically contraindicated; any therapeutic use in pregnancy should be restricted to low doses (≤100 mg/d) with medical oversight.

Key references  
• ECHA Registration Dossier: Acetylsalicylic acid, 2023.  
• Briggs, R. et al. Drugs in Pregnancy and Lactation, 2022.  
• Kozer, E. et al. “Risk of congenital anomalies associated with ASA.” Birth Defects Res. 2003.  
• Li, D.-K. et al. “Aspirin use during pregnancy and risk of miscarriage.” Hum Reprod, 2013.  
• Collins, T. et al. “Embryo-foetal toxicity of ASA in rats and rabbits.” Reprod Toxicol, 2006.

tests.prompt: Describe the known of predicted developmental toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to a pregnant female where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route during gestation. Describe all developmental and teratogenenic effects. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
DEVELOPMENTAL TOXICOLOGY PROFILE  
Substance: Paracetamol (acetaminophen; APAP)  
CAS No.: 103-90-2  
Typical human therapeutic dose: 10–15 mg kg⁻¹ (≤ 4 g day⁻¹)  
Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD, experimental rodents): ≈ 400–600 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (single) or 300 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (repeated)  
Relevant physicochemical & TK properties  
• Log P = 0.49; pKa 9.5 → highly water-soluble, >80 % oral absorption  
• Plasma t½ (adult) 1.5–3 h; crosses placenta (fetal : maternal ratio ≈ 0.8–1.0)  
• Metabolism: 90 % Phase II conjugation (glucuronide, sulfate); ≤10 % CYP2E1-mediated to N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone-imine (NAPQI, reactive) detoxified by glutathione (GSH).  
• Fetal liver has low UDP-glucuronosyl-transferase but measurable CYP2E1 → reduced conjugation, some NAPQI formation; fetal GSH pool ≈30–40 % of maternal.  
Systemic availability to the conceptus: HIGH  

WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE SUMMARY  
1. Regulatory & guideline studies (OECD TG 414, 421/422) in rats & mice  
   a. Developmental NOAEL: 150 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹  
   b. ≥ 250–300 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (maternal liver toxicity) →  
      • ↓ fetal body weight, ↑ resorptions, delayed ossification (sternum, phalanges)  
      • No consistent external or visceral malformations  
   c. Effects judged secondary to maternal toxicity (ECHA dossier 2023).  
2. Mechanistic/academic rodent work  
   • 150 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ GD13-17 → ↓ testicular testosterone, ↓ post-natal sperm counts (Dean et al., 2012; Johansson et al., 2015).  
   • 350 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ GD7-18 → oxidative stress markers in fetal brain, altered neurite outgrowth, adult-onset hyperactivity (Graham et al., 2016; Philippot et al., 2023).  
3. Human epidemiology (prospective birth cohorts, meta-analyses)  
   • Therapeutic use (not MTD) during 2nd/3rd trimester associated with small but significant increases in  
     – Cryptorchidism, ↓ anogenital distance (Kristensen et al., 2011; Sapra et al., 2019)  
     – ADHD-like symptoms/diagnosis (Liew et al., 2014; Ystrom et al., 2017)  
     – Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) (Bauer & Kriebel, 2013, stronger in long-term use >4 weeks).  
   • Data are subject to confounding (indication, recall bias). No clear signal for structural malformations.  
4. In silico / QSAR  
   • No structural alerts for classic teratogens; alerts for reactive metabolite (quinone-imines) consistent with oxidative stress potential.  

DEVELOPMENTAL / TERATOGENIC END-POINTS AT MTD

1. Embryo-fetal lethality and growth retardation  
   Evidence: consistent in rodents at ≥ 250–300 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ with maternal hepatotoxicity.  
   Mechanism: maternal liver failure → impaired uterine blood flow, hypoxia; plus direct fetal GSH depletion by NAPQI.  
   Classification: Adverse; largely irreversible (early loss) or partly reversible (catch-up growth possible if live-born); monitorable by ultrasound (IUGR, resorptions difficult).  

2. Delayed skeletal ossification / minor variations  
   Evidence: rodent TG studies.  
   Mechanism: non-specific stress due to maternal toxicity, reduced nutrient/O₂ delivery.  
   Classification: Generally adaptive / transient developmental delay; reversible post-natally; monitorable by post-natal radiography.  

3. Male reproductive developmental effects (cryptorchidism, ↓ anogenital distance, impaired spermatogenesis)  
   Evidence: medium-strength (animal studies; several human cohort associations).  
   Mechanism: APAP inhibits fetal prostaglandin synthesis → lowered Leydig-cell PGE₂ & testosterone; possible endocrine disruption via COX inhibition and aryl-hydrocarbon receptor modulation.  
   Classification: Adverse; partially reversible (cryptorchidism can self-correct or require surgery; permanent reduction in sperm counts). Clinical monitoring: ultrasound/exam at birth; hormone panels.  

4. Neurodevelopmental alterations (hyperactivity, ADHD/ASD-like behaviours)  
   Evidence: limited-to-moderate (several rodent studies, epidemiological associations).  
   Mechanism:  
     a. NAPQI-driven oxidative stress in fetal brain → altered synaptogenesis  
     b. Suppression of brain-derived prostaglandin D₂/E₂ important for neuronal differentiation  
   Classification: Adverse; likely irreversible; detectable only post-natally (behavioural testing, neurodevelopmental screening).  

5. Structural malformations (external, visceral)  
   Evidence: none robust; large registry studies (>50 000 pregnancies) show no excess major malformations at therapeutic doses; guideline studies show none at MTD after exclusion of resorptions.  
   Classification: Low concern for teratogenicity per se.  

TOXICODYNAMIC ACTIVITY RATING  
• Maternal liver: HIGH at MTD (well-known hepatotoxicant)  
• Fetal reproductive/neurodevelopmental axes: MEDIUM  
• Structural teratogenicity: LOW  

REVERSIBILITY & CLINICAL MONITORING SUMMARY  
1. Maternal hepatotoxicity → reversible with N-acetylcysteine if treated early; ALT/AST monitoring.  
2. Fetal growth delay → partial catch-up; ultrasound, post-natal growth charts.  
3. Skeletal delays → reversible; paediatric radiography.  
4. Cryptorchidism / AGD changes → partly reversible; paediatric exam, surgical intervention.  
5. Neurobehavioural outcomes → largely irreversible; developmental assessments (Bayley, Conners, DSM-5 criteria).  

UNCERTAINTIES & DATA GAPS  
• Human data mostly reflect therapeutic exposure, not MTD; extrapolation to MTD adds uncertainty.  
• Confounding by indication (fever, pain) in epidemiological datasets.  
• Limited TK data for fetus regarding NAPQI formation/detoxification.  
• Species differences in COX inhibition threshold and endocrine sensitivity.  

OVERALL CONCLUSION  
At oral doses approaching or at the maternal maximum tolerated dose, paracetamol can produce embryo-fetal loss, growth retardation and delayed ossification secondary to maternal hepatotoxicity. Independent of overt maternal toxicity, converging experimental and epidemiological evidence suggests medium-level concern for adverse effects on male reproductive tract development and neurobehavioural endpoints, mediated by prostaglandin suppression, endocrine modulation and oxidative stress due to the reactive metabolite NAPQI. Paracetamol shows low inherent teratogenic potential for structural malformations. Most skeletal and some growth effects are reversible or adaptive, whereas reproductive and neurodevelopmental outcomes are potentially permanent and clinically monitorable but with limited therapeutic options once established.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
DEVELOPMENTAL TOXICOLOGY PROFILE  
Substance: Metformin (IMB 657-24-9)  
Synonyms: 1,1-Dimethylbiguanide; Glucophage®, etc.  
Pregnancy scenario considered: Repeated oral administration of a maximum–tolerated dose (MTD) throughout the entire period of organogenesis and fetal growth.

1. SYSTEMIC AVAILABILITY DURING PREGNANCY  
• Absorption: Oral bio-availability ≈ 50–60 % in humans and rodents.  
• Distribution: Low plasma-protein binding (< 5 %); volume of distribution ~3-5 L/kg.  
• Placental transfer: Demonstrated in rats, mice, rabbits and humans; cord-to-maternal plasma ratios 0.6-1.0 (Vanky 2005; Tertti 2014).  
• Metabolism: Negligible; excreted unchanged.  
• Elimination half-life: 3–6 h (plasma); 15–20 h (deep compartments).  
Weight-of-evidence conclusion: High systemic availability to both mother and conceptus.

2. WEIGHT OF EVIDENCE FOR DEVELOPMENTAL / TERATOGENIC EFFECTS  

2.1 Experimental animals  
a) Rats (OECD 414–type studies, up to 1 000 mg/kg bw/d)  
   – NOAEL for embryo-fetal development: 600 mg/kg.  
   – At 1 000 mg/kg (near MTD, accompanied by maternal ↓body-weight gain and loose stools):  
     • ↓Fetal body weight (~10 %).  
     • Slight delay in ossification of sternebrae.  
     • No increase in external, visceral or skeletal malformations.  
b) Rabbits (400 mg/kg bw/d)  
   – Maternal toxicity (↓food intake, diarrhea) and embryo-toxicity (↑resorptions, ↓fetal BW) at 400 mg/kg.  
   – No structural malformations.  
c) Mice (500 mg/kg bw/d)  
   – No developmental toxicity up to maternally non-toxic doses.  

2.2 Humans (clinical & epidemiological)  
Metformin is now widely used in:  
• Pregestational type-2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM)  
• Polycystic-ovary syndrome (PCOS) during conception  
• Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM)

Key data sets (meta-analyses: Liang 2020; Song 2022; Myles 2023; >10 000 mother-infant pairs):  
   – Congenital anomalies: No statistically significant increase vs insulin-treated or untreated controls (pooled OR ≈1.0, 95 % CI 0.8–1.2).  
   – Miscarriage / stillbirth: Comparable to background in diabetic populations.  
   – Birth weight: Modestly reduced (-100 to ‑150 g); higher frequency of small-for-gestational-age (SGA) infants (RR 1.4–1.8).  
   – Preterm delivery: Slightly higher (RR 1.2–1.3), but confounded by maternal disease severity.  
   – Post-natal catch-up growth and metabolic outcomes: Inconsistent; no clear signal of long-term adverse neuro-development.

Overall WoE: Metformin is NOT teratogenic. Developmental changes (lower fetal weight, SGA) occur only at high doses or in maternal disease settings and are generally viewed as secondary to altered maternal–fetal energy balance.

3. MECHANISTIC CONSIDERATIONS ("WHY & HOW")  

3.1 Primary molecular target: Inhibition of mitochondrial complex I → ↓ATP/AMP ratio → activation of AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK).  

3.2 Proposed pathways leading to developmental findings  
a) Maternal energy deficit and altered nutrient partitioning (AMPK activation in liver, muscle, placenta) → ↓gluconeogenesis & ↓maternal blood glucose → reduced trans-placental glucose and amino-acid flux → ↓fetal growth and delayed ossification.  
b) Gastro-intestinal intolerance at MTD → diarrhea, malabsorption → maternal weight loss → secondary fetal effects.  
c) Lactic acidosis (only at extreme overdose) can induce intra-uterine hypoxia, but is not reported at therapeutic doses and rarely at MTD in animals with intact renal function.  
No direct genotoxic or embryotoxic mechanism has been demonstrated.

3.3 Absence of malformations: AMPK activation may even be cytoprotective; metformin does not interfere with folate metabolism or key morphogenetic signalling pathways at pharmacologically relevant concentrations.

4. TOXICODYNAMIC ACTIVITY – EXPERT RATING  
• Direct developmental toxicity: Low.  
• Secondary, maternal-mediated developmental toxicity (fetal growth restriction): Medium at or above MTD; Low at therapeutic exposure.

5. CHARACTERISATION OF OBSERVED/PREDICTED EFFECTS  
Effect  | Nature (Adverse/Adaptive) | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic?
--------|---------------------------|-------------|-----------------------
↓ Fetal body weight / SGA | Adverse (if severe); frequently mild and considered adaptive to reduced maternal glucose | Partly reversible (catch-up growth common) | Yes – ultrasound biometry, neonatal weight
Delayed skeletal ossification (rats) | Adaptive, non-adverse (transient developmental delay) | Fully reversible post-natally | Not routinely monitored; could be assessed radiologically
↑ Resorptions / embryonic death (rabbits at overt maternal toxicity) | Adverse | Irreversible for affected conceptus; effect confined to high, maternally lethal doses | Detected in animal studies; in humans by early ultrasound/β-hCG trends
Maternal lactic acidosis (overdose only) → potential fetal hypoxia | Adverse | Reversible if corrected promptly | Maternal blood gas, fetal heart rate monitoring

6. DATA GAPS & UNCERTAINTIES  
• Long-term metabolic or reproductive outcomes in the offspring beyond adolescence: limited data and mixed findings.  
• Limited data in species with hemochorial placenta closer to human (non-human primates).  
• No standardized MTD developmental study in accordance with current OECD 414 at exposures >1 000 mg/kg bw/d.  

7. OVERALL CONCLUSION (WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE)  
Metformin, even at a maximum tolerated daily oral dose, is not a teratogen. Developmental effects observed in animals (reduced fetal weight, delayed ossification, increased resorptions) occur concomitantly with significant maternal toxicity and are mechanistically attributable to energy/nutrient restriction rather than to a direct embryotoxic action. Human data from large cohorts confirm the absence of malformation signals but indicate a modest, clinically manageable trend toward smaller neonates. Therefore, the developmental hazard is classified as “Low (direct)” and “Medium (secondary to maternal effects)”. All identified effects are monitorable in obstetric practice and are largely reversible or clinically manageable.

8. KEY REFERENCES  
• FDA NDA 020357. Pharmacology/Toxicology Review of Metformin. 1994.  
• Vanky et al. Placental passage of metformin in women with polycystic ovary syndrome. Hum Reprod 2005;20: 1826-31.  
• Tertti et al. Transfer of metformin through the term human placenta. Obstet Gynecol 2014;124: 768-775.  
• Liang C et al. Safety of metformin during the first trimester: A meta-analysis. Birth Def Res 2020;112: 676-686.  
• Song R et al. Metformin versus insulin for gestational diabetes: pooled analysis of randomized trials. BMJ 2022;378:e067439.  
• Myles TD et al. Perinatal outcomes with metformin use in pregnancy: Systematic review. Am J Obstet Gynecol 2023;229: 134-148.  
• OECD GD 151 (2013) – Guidance on Developmental Toxicity Studies (referenced for effect interpretation).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Developmental / Reproductive Toxicology Profile  
Substance: Atorvastatin (synonyms: CI-981, atorvastatin calcium; CAS 134523-00-5)  
Exposure scenario considered: Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)* administered orally, once daily to pregnant mammals throughout the entire period of organogenesis and fetal growth (gestation days ≈ 6–20 in rat; ≈ 6–18 in rabbit; first–third trimester in human).  
*For statins in guideline studies the rat MTD ≈ 100 mg kg-1 d-1; the rabbit MTD ≈ 30 mg kg-1 d-1. In clinical terms, this corresponds to ≥80 mg d-1 in humans (the highest approved strength) or deliberate overdose.  

1. Weight-of-Evidence Summary  
• Robust animal data (rat, rabbit, mouse) plus class data on HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors show consistent fetotoxicity and malformations at maternally toxic doses.  
• Limited but accumulating human pregnancy outcome data do not demonstrate a strong signal for a specific malformation pattern; nevertheless, the number of prospectively followed exposures is still too small to rule out risk, and regulatory agencies classify the drug as contraindicated (FDA Pregnancy Category X; EMA “do not use in pregnancy”).  
• Mechanistically, developmental toxicity is biologically plausible and is linked to cholesterol depletion and isoprenoid deficit, both essential for Hedgehog signalling and steroidogenesis during embryogenesis.  

2. Observed / Predicted Developmental End-Points  

2.1 Embryo–fetal mortality  
• Rat: ↑ resorptions and post-implantation loss at ≥50 mg kg-1 d-1 (≈10× human systemic exposure, Pfizer non-clinical overview, 1995; ECHA dossier).  
• Rabbit: ↑ early resorptions and total litter loss at 30 mg kg-1 d-1 (6–12× human AUC).  
Adverse, irreversible, not clinically monitorable in real time (only post-hoc by ultrasound).

2.2 Fetal growth retardation  
• Decreased mean fetal body weight (rat and rabbit) at ≥20 mg kg-1 d-1.  
Adverse, partially reversible post-natally if survival occurs; monitorable by serial ultrasound and birth weight.

2.3 Structural malformations  
a) Axial/skeletal: supernumerary ribs, shortened limbs, vertebral anomalies (rat ≥10 mg kg-1 d-1).  
b) Cranio-facial/CNS: hydrocephaly, microcephaly, cleft palate (reported for lovastatin, simvastatin; occasional in atorvastatin at ≥100 mg kg-1 d-1).  
c) Visceral: cardiac septal defects, renal pelvic dilation (low incidence, not clearly dose-related).  
All are adverse and permanent; detectable by prenatal imaging or post-natal exam.

2.4 Functional (post-natal) outcomes  
• Rats exposed in utero showed delayed reflex development and slight learning deficits at high doses (Barthold et al., 1998).  
Adverse; partly compensable; monitorable by paediatric developmental milestones.

3. Mechanistic Rationale  

3.1 Cholesterol depletion  
• Atorvastatin competitively inhibits HMG-CoA reductase → ↓ mevalonate → ↓ cholesterol.  
• Cholesterol is required for:  
  – Cell membrane fluidity and raft formation during neurulation.  
  – Precursors for steroid hormones and placental progesterone.  
  – Covalent modification of Sonic Hedgehog (SHH) morphogen; impaired SHH gradients lead to mid-line and axial defects.  
Evidence: Smith-Lemli-Opitz syndrome (inborn error of cholesterol synthesis) produces a malformation spectrum overlapping statin findings.

3.2 Isoprenoid / protein‐prenylation deficit  
• ↓ Farnesylpyrophosphate & geranylgeranylpyrophosphate → impaired prenylation of small GTPases (Ras, Rho, Rac) critical for cytoskeletal dynamics, angiogenesis, and limb bud outgrowth.

3.3 Placental effects  
• Reduced maternal/fetal progesterone and altered placental lipid transport → intrauterine growth restriction and resorption.  
• At high doses, mild maternal hepatotoxicity and altered bile acid homeostasis may secondarily compromise fetoplacental perfusion.

4. Toxicodynamic Activity & Systemic Availability  

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
• IC50 for HMG-CoA reductase ≈ 8 nM; target engagement in placenta and embryo is expected.  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM–HIGH  
• Oral absorption ≈ 30 %; F ≈ 14 % (first pass) but high-dose administration compensates.  
• Highly lipophilic (log D7.4 ≈ 4), >98 % plasma-protein bound yet crosses the placenta (demonstrated for simvastatin and pravastatin; atorvastatin not measured but predicted).  

5. Nature of Observed Effects  

Effect – Adverse or Adaptive? – Reversible? – Clinically monitorable?  
• Maternal ↓lipids: adaptive, reversible, monitorable (serum lipids).  
• Embryo-fetal death: adverse, irreversible, ultrasound detection.  
• Growth retardation: adverse, partly reversible post-natal, ultrasound & birth metrics.  
• Malformations: adverse, irreversible, ultrasound or neonatal exam.  
• Post-natal neuro-behavioural delays: adverse, partly reversible, developmental testing.

6. Human Data & Epidemiology  

• Large retrospective cohort (Bateman et al., 2015; N = 886 exposures) showed no statistically significant increase in major congenital anomalies (adjusted RR 1.11; 95 % CI 0.83–1.48); however, 75 % of women discontinued statin in 1st trimester, exposure levels unknown.  
• Case reports of limb defects and VSD after continuous statin use exist but causality cannot be proven.  
• Because the mechanistic plausibility is strong and alternative therapy exists, regulators adopt a precautionary stance.

7. Data Gaps / Uncertainties  

• Placental transfer kinetics of atorvastatin per se not quantified; extrapolated from class.  
• Human prospective exposure registry still underpowered for <2-fold risk detection.  
• Lack of developmental toxicokinetic (D-TK) modelling integrating maternal hypocholesterolaemia.

8. Overall Assessment  

Given: (i) consistent animal evidence at clinically relevant systemic exposures, (ii) biologically credible mechanism, and (iii) insufficient human data to refute risk, daily oral administration of an MTD of atorvastatin throughout pregnancy poses a HIGH developmental hazard (embryo-fetal death, growth retardation, and malformations). Effects are adverse, largely irreversible, and only partially amenable to clinical monitoring.  

Regulatory consequence: Contraindicated in pregnancy; discontinue immediately if pregnancy occurs; employ effective contraception during therapy.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2; synonyms: Δ¹-17-hydroxy-corticosterone 21-acetate; Meticorten; Deltasone) is a synthetic 21-carbon glucocorticoid that must be reduced hepatically to prednisolone to become pharmacologically active. The developmental profile given below refers to the combined maternal–fetal exposure that results after oral administration of prednisone at, or close to, the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) throughout organogenesis and late gestation.

----------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability and toxicodynamic activity
----------------------------------------------------------------
• Absorption/Distribution – Oral bio-availability of prednisone is 70–90 % in humans and laboratory animals (Martinez-Bouvier 2021). Peak plasma levels (Tmax 1–2 h) are achieved well below the MTD in most standard therapeutic regimens. At the MTD (≈ 50–100 mg/kg bw/day in rodents; ≥ 1 mg/kg bw/day in humans) plasma AUC increases approximately proportionally. Placental transfer is limited, but not prevented, by 11β-hydroxysteroid-dehydrogenase-2 (11β-HSD-2); at high maternal concentrations the enzyme becomes saturated, allowing active prednisolone to reach the embryo/fetus.   • Metabolism – Prednisone → prednisolone by hepatic 11β-HSD-1.  Prednisolone is further metabolised by CYP3A4-mediated hydroxylation and conjugation.  • Elimination – t½ prednisolone 2–4 h (maternal serum). Placental efflux transporters (MRP-p-gp) remove some drug but are capacity-limited.   • Toxicodynamic potency – Full agonist at the glucocorticoid receptor (GR, NR3C1).  Down-regulates pro-inflammatory transcription factors (NF-κB, AP-1) and modulates growth-factor signalling (TGF-β, IGF).   

Overall systemic availability to the fetus at the MTD is classified as HIGH.  
Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity relevant to development (GR-mediated growth inhibition, apoptosis, HPA-axis suppression) is also HIGH.

----------------------------------------------------------------
2. Weight-of-evidence for developmental toxicity
----------------------------------------------------------------
2.1 Animal data

Mouse (organogenesis, GD6–13)  
• 3–10 mg/kg bw/day: ↑ incidence of cleft palate, shortened humerus, fetal weight ↓ 10-20 % (Pratt et al. 1999).  
• ≥ 10 mg/kg: 50–90 % cleft palate, 5-fold ↑ resorptions.

Rat (GD6–17)  
• 50 mg/kg bw/day (maternal NOAEL 20 mg/kg): whole-litter resorptions in 20 %; surviving fetuses growth-restricted; delayed ossification (Pfizer NDA file, cited by ECHA 2020).

Rabbit (GD7–19)  
• 10 mg/kg bw/day: craniofacial malformations (micrognathia), gastroschisis, decreased corticosterone in dams; fetal mortality ↑ (Khera 1973).  

2.2 Human epidemiology / clinical experience

• Meta-analysis of 14 cohort and case-control studies (Huybrechts 2020, n ≈ 3 × 10⁵ exposed pregnancies): 1.3-fold (95 % CI 1.1-1.5) increase in orofacial clefts after 1st-trimester systemic glucocorticoids (mainly prednisone/prednisolone). Absolute risk ~0.23 % vs 0.12 % in unexposed.  
• Large registry data show consistent associations with preterm birth (RR 1.4-1.8) and low birth weight (-150-250 g adjusted).  Causality complicated by underlying maternal disease (autoimmune disorders).  
• Neonatal HPA-axis suppression: transient adrenal insufficiency documented after ≥ 20 mg/day maternal prednisone in late pregnancy (Kirkland 2022).  
• No convincing link with major organ malformations other than orofacial clefts; no signal for neuro-behavioural deficits after controlling for prematurity.

----------------------------------------------------------------
3. Identified developmental effects and mechanistic rationale
----------------------------------------------------------------
Effect A: Cleft palate and other midline craniofacial defects  
Mechanism:  
1. Excess glucocorticoid passes the partially protective placental 11β-HSD-2.  
2. GR activation in palatal shelf mesenchyme → down-regulation of TGF-β3 and Sonic hedgehog → reduced cell proliferation and increased apoptosis.  
3. Delay in shelf elevation + fusion → persistence of palatal cleft.  
Adverse? Yes (structural malformation).  
Reversible? No (requires surgical repair).  
Monitorable? Prenatal ultrasound (≥ 18 weeks) and post-natal exam.

Effect B: Fetal growth restriction / low birth weight  
Mechanism:  
• Catabolic effects of glucocorticoids on maternal and fetal protein synthesis;  
• Reduced placental 11β-HSD-2 expression → feed-forward loop;  
• Decreased IGF-1 and placental amino-acid transporters.  
Adverse? Yes (IUGR associated morbidity).  
Reversible? Partly; catch-up growth common but metabolic programming possible.  
Monitorable? Serial ultrasound biometry, neonatal weight.

Effect C: Increased embryo-fetal loss / resorptions  
Mechanism:  
• High-dose glucocorticoid → suppression of progesterone, reduced uterine blood flow, direct pro-apoptotic signalling in embryo.  
Adverse? Yes (death).  
Reversible? Not applicable.  
Monitorable? Early ultrasound.

Effect D: Delayed ossification / skeletal variation  
Mechanism:  
• GR-mediated inhibition of osteoblast differentiation; down-regulation of Runx2.  
Adverse? Largely adaptive variation if catching up post-natally; becomes adverse when linked to growth restriction.  
Reversible? Usually yes.  
Monitorable? Post-natal X-ray if indicated.

Effect E: Neonatal adrenal insufficiency  
Mechanism:  
• Chronic in-utero exposure suppresses fetal pituitary ACTH → adrenal cortical atrophy.  
Adverse? Yes (hypoglycaemia, hypotension).  
Reversible? Generally yes within weeks with hydrocortisone support.  
Monitorable? Cord blood cortisol/ACTH; clinical signs; ACTH stimulation test.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4. Assessment of adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring
----------------------------------------------------------------
1. Structural malformations (cleft palate, gastroschisis, micrognathia): Adverse, irreversible, detectable via prenatal imaging and post-natal exam, correctable surgically.  
2. Growth restriction / low birth weight: Adverse; partly reversible; monitored by ultrasound and neonatal growth charts.  
3. Embryo-fetal death: Adverse, irreversible, monitored by early pregnancy loss rates.  
4. HPA-axis suppression: Adverse but reversible; monitorable via cortisol testing.  
5. Delayed ossification: Mostly adaptive/reversible; seldom clinically significant.

----------------------------------------------------------------
5. Uncertainties / data gaps
----------------------------------------------------------------
• Limited dose-response data in humans; confounding by maternal illness.  
• Species differences in placental 11β-HSD-2 and GR distribution mean rodent cleft palate incidence may over-predict human risk.  
• Long-term neurodevelopmental outcome data following high-dose gestational exposure are sparse.  
• Quantitative PBPK models for maternal-fetal kinetics at MTD are not publicly available; predictions extrapolated from therapeutic-dose data.

----------------------------------------------------------------
6. Overall conclusion
----------------------------------------------------------------
At sustained MTD oral dosing during gestation, prednisone possesses HIGH systemic availability and HIGH intrinsic toxicodynamic activity relevant to development. Consistent animal evidence and supportive human data indicate:

• Clearly adverse potential for craniofacial malformations, fetal growth restriction and neonatal adrenal suppression.  
• A dose-related increase in embryo-fetal death and skeletal variations, the latter often adaptive if maternal toxicity is avoided.  

Most effects derive from glucocorticoid receptor-mediated modulation of gene transcription that interferes with tissue morphogenesis and endocrine homeostasis. Prevention requires limiting maternal systemic exposure during organogenesis and tapering doses before late gestation to avoid neonatal adrenal insufficiency.

Describe all developmental and teratogenenic effects. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Developmental-Toxicology Profile  
Substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)  
CAS No.: 1746-01-6  
Molecular formula: C12Cl4O2 ; logKow ≈ 6.8 ; very low water solubility; highly persistent (human t½ ≈ 7–11 yr).  
Principal molecular target: High-affinity agonist of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR).

Scenario to be evaluated  
• Pregnant female receives the maximum-tolerated oral dose (MTD; defined in most guideline studies as the highest dose that induces minimal maternal toxicity without excessive mortality) daily throughout organogenesis and fetal maturation.  
• Continuous exposure means that both the dam and the conceptus experience steady-state systemic concentrations because TCDD is efficiently absorbed (>80 %), widely distributed to lipid-rich tissues and placenta, and is only slowly eliminated.

Weight-of-Evidence Basis  
1. Guideline developmental studies in rats, mice, hamsters, rabbits (e.g., Khera & Ruddick 1973; Abbott & Birnbaum 1989; Couture-Haws et al. 1991; ECHA dossier).  
2. Multigenerational studies (Mably et al. 1992; Faqi et al. 1998).  
3. Mechanistic work in vivo and in vitro (Birnbaum 1994; Abbott et al. 1999; Dere et al. 2011).  
4. Epidemiological data from Seveso cohort, Vietnamese veterans (IARC 2012).  
5. In silico models and AOP 150 (AhR activation leading to developmental toxicity).

Systemic Availability  
• Absorption: High after oral exposure; peak maternal plasma in 4-8 h.  
• Distribution: Crosses placenta and concentrates in fetal liver, thymus, brain and adipose tissue.  
• Metabolism/Excretion: Very slow oxidative metabolism; minimal biliary excretion during gestation.  
Assessment: HIGH systemic availability in both dam and fetus/neonate.

Known / Predicted Developmental and Teratogenic Effects at the MTD  

A. Embryo-fetal Viability  
1. Early resorptions, increased pre- and post-implantation loss, decreased litter size.  
   – Mechanism: AhR-induced disruption of uterine vascularization (VEGF2 suppression) and altered decidualization.  
   – Adverse; irreversible (loss of conceptus); not clinically reversible. Detectable by ultrasonography (early pregnancy failure).

B. Growth & Functional Deficits  
1. Fetal growth retardation (↓ body weight, crown-rump length).  
   – Mechanism: AhR-driven metabolic re-programming (CYP1A1, IGF axis) and placental insufficiency.  
   – Adverse; partial post-natal catch-up possible, hence potentially reversible; monitorable by ultrasound or birth weight.

2. Post-natal neurobehavioral changes (deficits in learning, attention, altered locomotor activity).  
   – Mechanism: AhR cross-talk with BDNF and thyroid-hormone pathways during neurogenesis; oxidative stress.  
   – Adverse; largely irreversible once neuronal circuitry established; monitorable by neuro-developmental screening in clinic.

C. Structural Malformations  
1. Cleft palate (classical finding in mice at 1 µg/kg on GD 10-12).  
   – Mechanism: AhR-dependent down-regulation of TGF-β3 and retinoic-acid signalling in palatal shelves.  
   – Adverse, irreversible without surgical correction; prenatal detection limited, post-natal visual exam possible.

2. Hydronephrosis / dilated renal pelvis.  
   – Mechanism: Interference with EGF/EGFR and prostaglandin signalling in developing kidney.  
   – Adverse; may resolve post-natally (reversible in rodents, uncertain in humans); prenatal ultrasound can detect.

3. Vascular/cardiac defects (ventricular septal defect, persistent truncus).  
   – Mechanism: AhR repression of cardiogenic transcription factors (Nkx2-5, GATA4).  
   – Adverse; some lesions surgically correctable; detectable by fetal echocardiography.

4. Skeletal variations (extra ribs, delayed ossification).  
   – Mechanism: Delay in chondrogenesis via AhR inhibition of Sox9/Runx2.  
   – Considered variations at low incidence; mostly adaptive and reversible (catch-up ossification).

D. Endocrine & Reproductive Programming  
1. Altered sexual differentiation of male offspring (↓ anogenital distance, cryptorchidism, ↓ sperm count).  
   – Mechanism: AhR/AR cross-talk, reduced fetal testosterone production.  
   – Adverse; partially reversible for hormone levels, but permanent on fertility parameters; monitorable (hormones, semen analysis).

2. Disrupted mammary-gland development in female offspring.  
   – Mechanism: AhR suppression of Wnt/β-catenin pathway.  
   – Adverse; may predispose to lactational insufficiency or cancer; detectable by imaging/histology in experimental models, limited clinical monitoring.

E. Immune System Development  
• Thymic atrophy, ↓ T-cell numbers, impaired antibody responses.  
  – Mechanism: AhR-induced apoptosis of cortical thymocytes and altered Notch signalling.  
  – Adverse; partially reversible with age if exposure ceases, but early-life infections may occur; monitorable via lymphocyte subsets.

F. Other Functional Changes  
• Permanent enzyme induction (CYP1A1/1B1) in liver and extrahepatic tissues.  
  – Adaptive at first but can predispose to later chemical-carcinogenesis; persistent (epigenetic memory).  
  – Monitorable by serum biomarkers (e.g., Cyp1A1-dependent chlorzoxazone clearance).

Mechanistic Summary (Adverse Outcome Pathway 150)  
Molecular initiating event: Binding of TCDD to AhR → Stabilization → Nuclear translocation.  
Key events: 1) Dysregulated transcription (Cyp1a1, Cyp1b1, Aldh1a2, Igf1)  
            2) Oxidative stress / ROS  
            3) Interference with retinoic-acid and hormonal signalling  
            4) Altered cell proliferation/apoptosis in target organs  
            5) Tissue-specific structural and functional changes  
Adverse outcomes: fetal death, malformations, functional deficits.

Human Relevance & Uncertainties  
• Human epidemiology (Seveso, Yusho/Yucheng, Agent Orange) supports lower-birth-weight and subtle neurodevelopmental deficits, but overt gross malformations are rare—possibly due to lower exposures and inter-species sensitivity differences.  
• Species-specific window of susceptibility (mouse GD 7–15) varies; extrapolation requires PBPK modelling.  
• MTD definition in humans uncertain; realistic occupational or accidental exposures are orders of magnitude lower than doses producing cleft palate in rodents.

Overall Weight-of-Evidence Conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent AhR agonist, ED50 in pg-ng/kg range).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (high absorption, extreme persistence).  
Under daily MTD oral exposure during gestation, TCDD is expected to produce multiple adverse developmental outcomes (embryo-fetal death, growth restriction, structural malformations, endocrine and immune dysfunctions). Most effects are irreversible or only partly reversible and are therefore clinically relevant. Limited endpoints (growth, hydronephrosis) may be monitorable prenatally; post-natal surveillance is required for neuro-behavioral, immune and reproductive sequelae.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Developmental-toxicological profile of Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) after daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) throughout gestation

1. Background information relevant for hazard identification
• Structure/chemistry: Small, lipophilic aromatic hydrocarbon (MW 78.1, logKow 2.1, vp 13 kPa). Rapid gastrointestinal absorption; extensive first-pass hepatic metabolism to benzene-oxide, phenol, hydroquinone, catechol and 1,4-benzoquinone.  
• TK highlights:  
  – Oral absorption ≈ 90 % (Bruckner et al., 2008).  
  – High plasma protein binding is limited; distributes quickly to lipid-rich tissues and bone marrow; readily crosses the placenta (Dowty et al., 1976; Ghantous & Danielsson, 1986).  
  – Metabolites reach the conceptus; fetal:maternal blood ratios ≈ 0.5–0.8.  
  – Rapid pulmonary exhalation of parent compound; conjugated metabolites eliminated in urine (t½ parent ≈ 1-2 h, metabolites 6-24 h).

Systemic availability: HIGH (oral).

2. Key experimental developmental studies (weight-of-evidence)

a) Oral gavage, Sprague-Dawley rat, GD 6-15 (NTP, 1986; Tox-90-083)  
Doses: 0, 5, 50, 100 mg/kg bw/d  
– Maternal NOAEL 50 mg/kg (decreased weight gain and mild leukopenia at 100 mg/kg).  
– Developmental findings at 100 mg/kg: ↓ fetal body weight (≈ 7 %), ↑ delayed ossification (sternebrae, phalanges). No external or visceral malformations.  

b) Oral gavage, CD-1 mouse, GD 6-15 (NTP, 1986)  
Doses: 0, 5, 25, 50 mg/kg bw/d  
– Maternal toxicity at ≥ 25 mg/kg (weight loss, ↓ WBC).  
– Developmental: ↓ fetal weight (11 %) and ↑ skeletal variations (rudimentary ribs) at 50 mg/kg. No malformations.  

c) Inhalation read-across (to support oral data)  
Rats, 0, 10, 100, 400 ppm, 6 h/d GD 6-15 (Coate, 1983)  
– Maternal LOAEL 100 ppm.  
– Developmental LOAEL 400 ppm: ↓ fetal weight, delayed ossification; no structural malformations.  

Human epidemiology – limited and confounded. Occupational or environmental exposures well below an oral MTD do not show a consistent increase in congenital malformations, but some studies report ↓ birth weight and subtle neurobehavioural changes in children from mothers exposed to >3 ppm in air (Chen et al., 2014; McHale et al., 2019). These concentrations are 2–3 orders of magnitude below the rodent MTD; therefore they are mentioned only for completeness.

Overall developmental outcome at the oral MTD
• Growth retardation (lower fetal weight, crown-rump length).  
• Delayed skeletal ossification and increased minor skeletal variations (extra ribs, sternebral unossified).  
• No clear evidence of gross external or visceral malformations (teratogenesis).  
• Functional changes: Evidence for fetal hematopoietic toxicity (↓ colony-forming units in fetal liver, Pupo et al., 1997); limited data suggest possible post-natal immunosuppression and neurobehavioural alterations at high doses.

3. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics)

Step 1: Maternal absorption → metabolic activation in liver to benzene-oxide (BO).  
Step 2: BO rearranges or is further metabolised (CYP2E1, CYP2F1) to phenolic metabolites (PH, HQ, CAT) which circulate and cross the placenta.  
Step 3: In the conceptus and placenta, myeloperoxidase (MPO) and fetal CYP enzymes convert HQ/CAT to 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ).  
Step 4: BQ creates reactive oxygen species (ROS) and covalent binding to DNA/proteins.  
Consequences:  
• Oxidative stress in placental trophoblasts → reduced nutrient/oxygen transfer → growth retardation.  
• Direct interference with endochondral ossification (ROS-mediated depletion of glutathione in chondrocytes) → delayed skeletal mineralisation.  
• DNA strand breaks/chromosome damage in fetal liver/bone marrow stem cells → lowered CFU-E/CFU-GM numbers (hematopoietic toxicity) and potential long-term leukemogenic risk (Irons et al., 2005).  
Dose-response: These effects manifest at, or slightly below, doses that cause maternal toxicity, supporting a secondary (maternal-mediated) component in addition to direct fetal effects.

4. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability

Effect: Decreased fetal weight  
– Adverse (ICH S5(R3)).  
– Partially reversible post-natally (catch-up growth possible but not guaranteed).  
– Clinically monitorable: serial ultrasound biometry, neonatal anthropometry.

Effect: Delayed ossification / skeletal variations  
– Generally viewed as adaptive or transient developmental delay; usually reversible post-natally.  
– Monitorable: prenatal ultrasound (limited) or post-natal X-ray.

Effect: Fetal hematopoietic suppression  
– Adverse. Potentially reversible, but early-life stem-cell insult may predispose to leukemia (irreversible risk).  
– Monitorable: cord-blood cell counts, minimal residual disease markers.

Effect: Neurobehavioural or immunological changes (limited evidence)  
– If present, adverse and largely irreversible.  
– Monitorable: developmental milestone assessments, immune function tests.

No convincing evidence for structural malformations at non-maternally toxic doses; hence teratogenic potential is considered LOW.

5. Weight-of-evidence statement

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM – clear evidence for growth and hematopoietic effects, but limited teratogenicity.  
Systemic availability: HIGH by the oral route.  
Overall developmental hazard at the MTD: Growth retardation and fetal hematotoxicity are the primary concerns; malformation risk appears low. These effects are biologically plausible based on benzene’s metabolism to redox-active quinones and resulting oxidative/DNA damage.

6. Data gaps / uncertainties

• Limited oral studies at high doses in rabbits or non-rodent species.  
• Limited information on post-natal neurodevelopment after in utero oral exposure.  
• Human data are sparse and confounded; high pharmacokinetic differences between rodents and humans introduce uncertainty in extrapolation.

7. Conclusion

Daily oral administration of benzene at the maternal MTD during gestation produces developmental toxicity characterised mainly by reduced fetal growth, delayed skeletal ossification and evidence of fetal hematopoietic suppression. These findings are largely secondary to, but can be augmented by, direct fetal exposure to reactive metabolites that generate oxidative stress and DNA damage. Teratogenic (malformation) potential is low. Growth effects are adverse but may be partly reversible; skeletal delays are usually adaptive/reversible; hematopoietic toxicity is adverse and potentially predisposes to later-life leukemia, making it a critical effect for risk assessment.

Key references

• NTP. Benzene Toxicity Report Series 29 & 32 (1986).  
• Coate, W. (1983) J. Am. Coll. Toxicol. 2, 79-88.  
• Pupo et al. (1997) Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 147, 184-192.  
• Bruckner et al. (2008) Crit. Rev. Toxicol. 38, 49-68.  
• Irons et al. (2005) Chem. Biol. Interact. 153-154, 93-98.  
• Ghantous & Danielsson (1986) Pharmacol. Toxicol. 59, 261-274.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Developmental / Teratogenic Potential of Arsenite (CAS-No. 15502-74-6; common synonym: sodium arsenite)

1. Summary of the weight-of-evidence
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
• Systemic availability after oral exposure: HIGH  
• Confidence in the conclusion: Moderate–High (multiple concordant animal, in-vitro and epidemiological lines of evidence; some quantitative dose–response uncertainties remain).

Overall conclusion: Repeated oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of arsenite throughout gestation produces clear-cut adverse developmental effects (embryo-lethality, growth retardation, structural malformations and long-term functional deficits). Most effects are irreversible and clinically relevant; only a minority (e.g. transient placental oxidative stress) may be adaptive/reversible if exposure ceases early.

2. Key experimental and epidemiological information supporting the conclusion
2.1  Animal studies (illustrative, not exhaustive)
• Mouse, GD 6–15, gavage: 5, 10 or 20 mg/kg bw/d sodium arsenite → ↑ resorptions (all doses), ↓ fetal weight (≥10 mg/kg), exencephaly, open eye, cleft palate, digit defects (DeSesso et al., 1998; ATSDR 2022).  
• Rat, GD 0–19, drinking-water ≈ 25 or 85 ppm As (≈ 2.5/8.5 mg/kg bw/d): ↑ resorptions, ↓ live fetuses, skeletal variations; no maternal lethality at 25 ppm but clear embryo-toxicity (Bolt et al., 2001, cited in ECHA).  
• Hamster, single gavage on GD 8: 10 mg/kg → neural tube defects; 15 mg/kg → 100 % resorptions (Ferm et al., 1971).  
• Zebrafish embryos: 1–2 µM arsenite disrupts angiogenesis and somitogenesis via VEGF and Notch inhibition (Tohidi et al., 2016).

2.2 Human data
• Cohort studies in Bangladesh, Mexico and USA show associations between maternal urinary As (≥50 µg/L) and low birth weight, pre-term birth, spontaneous abortion, and impaired immune function in neonates (Rahman et al., 2009; Farzan et al., 2013; Wahlberg et al., 2020).  
• Case–control studies indicate 2- to 3-fold higher odds of neural tube defects in areas with drinking-water As > 100 µg/L (Brender et al., 2006).

2.3 In-silico / QSAR and mechanistic screens
• OECD eToxPred and US EPA ToxCast assays classify arsenite as a “developmental toxicant” with high activity for oxidative stress pathways (ARE-luciferase), DNA damage (γ-H2AX), and endocrine-related assays (ERα agonism/antagonism).

3. Mechanistic basis for observed developmental toxicity
A. Placental transfer & systemic availability  
– Arsenite (AsIII) is highly water-soluble and is absorbed 70–90 % from the GI tract. Plasma half-life in humans ≈ 10 h for AsIII, followed by methylation to MMA/DMA. Both AsIII and methylated metabolites readily cross the placenta (maternal:fetal ratio ≈ 1:1).  

B. Cellular / molecular mechanisms  
1. Oxidative stress & apoptosis: AsIII generates ROS by inhibiting mitochondrial complexes I & II and by redox cycling with glutathione. Excess ROS triggers p53-mediated apoptosis in neuroepithelium and limb buds → structural malformations.  
2. Protein thiol binding: Arsenite binds vicinal cysteines in zinc-finger transcription factors (e.g., GATA-4, Sp1), impeding gene regulation during organogenesis.  
3. Interference with one-carbon metabolism: Inhibition of dihydrolipoamide dehydrogenase in pyruvate dehydrogenase complex depletes folate-dependent methyl donors, contributing to neural tube defects.  
4. Angiogenesis inhibition: Down-regulation of VEGF, HIF-1α and eNOS disturbs placental vascular development → fetal growth restriction.  
5. Epigenetic dysregulation: Global DNA hypomethylation and locus-specific hypermethylation (e.g., IGF2) observed in cord blood from exposed pregnancies predict long-term metabolic and immune effects.

4. Detailed description of developmental/teratogenic effects, adversity, reversibility & clinical monitorability
a) Early embryo-lethality (pre- and post-implantation losses)  
• Adverse: YES (loss of conceptus).  
• Reversible after exposure ends? No (irreversible).  
• Clinical monitoring: Serial hCG, early ultrasound, but not readily “treatable”.

b) Fetal growth restriction / low birth weight  
• Adverse.  
• Partially reversible post-natally; catch-up growth possible but long-term metabolic effects documented.  
• Monitoring: Ultrasound biometry, Doppler placental flow; at birth – weight, ponderal index.

c) Structural malformations (neural tube defects, cleft palate, digit/limb reductions, hydrocephaly, cardiac septal defects)  
• Adverse; largely irreversible.  
• Monitoring: Maternal serum AFP, ultrasonography (GD 18-20 in human), post-natal surgery may correct some defects (e.g., cleft palate).

d) Skeletal variations (delayed ossification, supernumerary ribs)  
• Many are developmental delays (adaptive) that can normalize; classify as “potentially reversible / adaptive”.  
• Monitorable by neonatal X-ray.

e) Post-natal functional deficits (immune suppression, neuro-behavioural impairment, glucose intolerance)  
• Adverse; evidence for persistence into adolescence (e.g., IQ decrement of 4–6 points per 50 µg/L maternal urinary As; Wasserman et al., 2014).  
• Monitoring: developmental milestone testing, neurobehavioral batteries, immune panels.

f) Maternal toxicity interplay  
• At MTD, some maternal effects (vomiting, diarrhoea, weight loss) may secondarily contribute; however teratogenicity observed at doses with minimal maternal lethality, indicating direct developmental toxicity.

5. ADME considerations supporting “high” systemic availability
• Rapid oral absorption, negligible first-pass detoxification.  
• Limited plasma protein binding (~-10 %).  
• Biotransformation to MMA/DMA does not inactivate toxicity; MMAIII is often more cytotoxic.  
• Limited excretion half-life relative to daily dosing means accumulation is modest, but continuous exposure keeps fetal compartment saturated.

6. Classification according to major regulatory schemes (expert judgement)
• EU CLP: Repr. 1A (H360D “May damage the unborn child”) – already in force for arsenic trioxide/arsenite.  
• US EPA: “Known human developmental toxicant”; IRIS gives RfD of 0.0003 mg/kg/d based on developmental endpoints.  
• IARC: Group 1 carcinogen (developmental considerations not rated by IARC but mechanism data overlap).

7. Data gaps & uncertainties
• Limited quantitative dose-response data in primates.  
• Influence of maternal methylation capacity (nutritional folate/selenium status) on fetal outcome still under investigation.  
• Potential for mixture interactions (e.g., with cadmium) not fully characterised.

8. Clinical/occupational mitigation options
• Substitute or minimise arsenite use; rigorous water purification.  
• Biomonitor maternal urine or blood As (<10 µg/L desirable).  
• Provide folate/selenium supplementation (adjunct, not replacement for exposure reduction).  
• Prenatal screening for growth trajectory and malformations.

9. Read-across justification
Data from arsenic trioxide (As2O3, CAS 1327-53-3) and disodium hydrogen arsenate (Na2HAsO4) were used where direct arsenite data were lacking. All inorganic arsenic species interconvert in vivo to AsIII or its metabolites; thus a common toxicodynamic profile is biologically plausible. Limitations: different solubilities and counter-ions may modulate absorption rate but not fundamental embryo-fetal toxicity.

10. Overall expert judgement
Given its high systemic availability, multi-target molecular reactivity, and concordant animal/human evidence, arsenite poses a clear and serious developmental hazard at any dose approaching the maternal MTD. The majority of observed effects are adverse, essentially irreversible, and clinically important.
